name: AcopioVzla
db_description:
Acopio nació como respuesta a un problema concreto: en situaciones de emergencia, la ayuda humanitaria se dispersa porque no existe información centralizada y actualizada sobre qué necesita cada centro de acopio. La comunicación ocurre por WhatsApp, el esfuerzo solidario se concentra en los centros más conocidos mientras otros quedan desabastecidos, y los voluntarios no saben a dónde ir ni qué llevar.
Acopio resuelve esto con una plataforma web simple: cada centro registra su perfil y publica actualizaciones en tiempo real sobre sus necesidades. La ciudadanía accede a un feed público donde puede ver, filtrar y decidir cómo ayudar de forma concreta e informada.
El proyecto es gratuito, sin publicidad y de acceso abierto. Fue construido con React, Vite, Supabase y PostGIS, con foco en ser liviano, rápido de adoptar y fácil de operar incluso desde un celular básico. Entre más centros participan, más útil se vuelve para todos — es infraestructura solidaria que escala con la comunidad.

analysis.summary: Acopio-VZLA (also named "saman" internally) is a fully functional React SPA for registering and browsing Venezuelan earthquake aid-collection centers (centros de acopio). The app is already live on Vercel. It uses Supabase as the entire backend (Auth, PostgreSQL+PostGIS, Storage, Realtime) and implements a Twitter/Instagram-style feed of updates per center, community posts, comments, notifications via DB trigger, shelter announcements, and photo upload with client-side compression. Codebase is domain-specific, Spanish-language, and was built rapidly but deliberately over ~2 days with 23 commits. Tests exist and achieve ~99% line coverage. No CI, no server-side code beyond Supabase SQL/RLS.
analysis.project_type: Frontend SPA (React/Vite) backed by Supabase BaaS

database: PostGIS (geom column + GIST index for KNN)

architecture.pattern: Feature-sliced SPA with BaaS (no custom server)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS SDK (@supabase/supabase-js) calling PostgREST and RPCs directly — no ORM. SQL logic lives in versioned PLpgSQL migrations and RPC functions (e.g. centros_cercanos using PostGIS KNN). RLS is the sole authorization layer.
architecture.api_design: No custom API server. All data access goes through Supabase PostgREST (table queries), Supabase RPC (centros_cercanos geo query), Supabase Realtime (postgres_changes), and Supabase Storage.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: src/features/{domain}/{queries,mutations,realtime}.ts holds data logic; src/components/ holds UI; src/pages/ holds route-level composition; src/lib/ holds singletons (supabase client, storage utils, geo). No feature logic bleeds into pages.
architecture.modularity: Well-modularized by domain feature (centros, posts, notificaciones, anuncios, auth). Components further split into layout, post, centro, common, ui, toast, notificacion, anuncio subdirectories.
notable_patterns: TanStack Query for all async state with cache invalidation on mutations
Optimistic updates implemented for post-util toggles
DB-level auto-notifications via PLpgSQL trigger (trg_notificar_comentario)
Realtime subscriptions scoped per-centro with proper useEffect cleanup
Client-side image compression before upload (canvas.toBlob quality loop)
RLS-enforced coordinador_id — never trusted from client payload
Nominatim geocoding with 1 req/sec throttle awareness for geocoding form fields

code_organization.directory_structure: Textbook feature-slice layout under src/features/, src/components/, src/pages/, src/lib/. Supabase migrations numbered sequentially (00001–00010). Test mocks isolated in src/test/mocks/. Docs in /docs with PRD.md and ARCHITECTURE.md.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistently domain-appropriate Spanish naming throughout (centros_acopio, coordinador_id, necesidades, notificacion, anuncio) matching the real Venezuelan context. TypeScript interfaces are explicit and match DB schema.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is detailed and accurate covering setup, migrations, testing, build, and security model. ARCHITECTURE.md and PRD.md provide additional product/technical context. Inline comments in migrations explain each section.
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is organized well beyond typical hackathon quality, with clear feature separation, proper migration versioning, and meaningful documentation. Committed coverage-summary.json with a Linux path (/home/imn0p/) is a minor artifact of rushed workflow.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: VITE_SUPABASE_URL and VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY in .env (gitignored). .env.example committed. The anon key is intentionally public per Supabase design; actual authorization is enforced by RLS.
production_readiness.security_notes: RLS enforced on all tables (centros_acopio, posts, post_comentario, notificacion, anuncio, anuncio_util) and Storage buckets. coordinador_id always derived from auth.uid() server-side. Notification trigger uses SECURITY DEFINER. No custom API surface to exploit.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: PostGIS KNN index for geo queries scales well. TanStack Query caching reduces redundant fetches. Realtime throttled to 2 events/second. Supabase free tier may hit connection limits under load; no connection pooling config visible.
production_readiness.reasoning: The app is genuinely deployed and functional with proper RLS security. Gaps are: no CI pipeline, no Dockerfile or explicit deploy config (Vercel auto-detects), no structured error logging/monitoring, and no rate-limiting on write mutations beyond Supabase defaults.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, purpose-built application. Domain-specific logic throughout (Venezuelan cities, earthquake relief terminology, PostGIS KNN for nearby centers, PLpgSQL notification triggers). Not boilerplate.
maturity.reasoning: 23 commits in ~2 days shows rapid but genuine development. Test coverage is impressively high (99% lines, 92% functions via Vitest+MSW) for a hackathon. The absence of any CI config and the committed coverage file with a developer's local Linux path are hallmarks of move-fast hackathon velocity.

viability.reasoning: The app is already live and usable at acopiovzla.vercel.app. The core value proposition — publicly browsable map of aid collection centers with real-time updates, coordinator-managed posts, community feed, and shelter announcements — directly addresses earthquake victim needs. The Supabase free tier can handle small-to-medium traffic; scaling to Supabase Pro is straightforward. The only real gap for sustained deployment is lack of monitoring and CI.

key_features: Aid collection center registry with PostGIS-powered nearby search
Coordinator-managed posts per center with photo upload (client-side compression)
Real-time feed updates via Supabase Realtime (postgres_changes)
Community feed for general posts not tied to a center
Comments on posts with DB-trigger-based notifications to coordinators
Shelter availability announcements (hospedaje type, extensible)
Public read without login; write requires Supabase Auth
Geocoding via Nominatim (OpenStreetMap) with manual fallback
Mobile-first layout with responsive desktop sidebar
Light/dark theme support
Needs tagging (necesidades) with predefined + custom chips
analysis.merge_potential: Low — this is a self-contained SPA with its own Supabase backend. It could be featured as a standalone tool or linked from a portal, but its architecture (client-only, Supabase BaaS) makes it difficult to merge with server-rendered or differently-architected projects without significant rework.
red_flags: No CI/CD pipeline — no automated test runs on push, no build verification before deploy
Committed coverage/coverage-summary.json with developer's local Linux path reveals rushed workflow
Single contributor — bus-factor of 1 with no review process
No error monitoring or logging (no Sentry, no structured logs)
Supabase free tier has row/storage/bandwidth limits that could be hit in a real emergency surge
No rate-limiting on write mutations (centro creation, post creation) beyond Supabase defaults — potential for spam/abuse in a public emergency context

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Venezuela's earthquake response suffers from a well-documented coordination failure: aid collection centers communicate needs via WhatsApp, causing some centers to be overwhelmed while others go unnoticed. AcopioVzla directly targets this gap with a real-time, publicly browsable registry of centros de acopio, coordinator-managed need updates, PostGIS-powered proximity search, and shelter announcements. The problem is concrete, the solution is appropriately scoped, and the domain logic throughout the codebase (Venezuelan city names, earthquake relief terminology, necesidades tagging) confirms this is purpose-built for the actual crisis, not a generic template.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The Vercel deployment at acopiovzla.vercel.app is confirmed live (signals.accessible: true, pushed 2026-06-29T17:49Z). WebFetch retrieved only the HTML shell title "Saman — Conectando Venezuela" because this is a client-side React SPA that requires JavaScript execution to render. The app is functionally deployed; WebFetch's inability to execute JS prevents visual confirmation of the rendered UI. No demo video is provided, so a reviewer cannot bypass the JS limitation with a recording.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Frame it as infrastructure solidaria: a free, open, real-time map connecting donors with the aid centers that actually need them — because in an earthquake, WhatsApp chaos costs lives. Highlight that any centro can register in minutes, needs update in real time, and anyone with a phone can find the nearest drop-off point.
gaps: No demo video — a 60-second screen recording would dramatically increase share rate
No screenshots for social posts — static images of the feed/map view are missing
No branding assets (logo, OG image) visible — social previews will be blank
No real-user adoption data yet — zero evidence of centros actually registered, which weakens social proof

evaluation.one_line_pitch: AcopioVzla conecta donantes con centros de acopio en tiempo real — porque en un terremoto, la información salva vidas.

name: AidTrace | RastroAyuda
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

Construí **AidTrace | RastroAyuda**, una app offline-first compatible para desktop, mobile y Telegram para rastrear ayuda humanitaria con QR y pruebas en Celo blockchain

## ¿Para quién es?

Para equipos humanitarios, refugios, donantes y auditores que necesitan verificar entregas de agua, comida, medicinas o kits de emergencia.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Permite registrar entregas, recepciones y revisiones incluso sin internet. Cuando vuelve la conexión, guarda la prueba en Celo y muestra el enlace en Celoscan.

Los usuarios en campo no necesitan wallet, CELO ni saber de blockchain.

## ¿Qué deberían probar primero?

Crear un lote, generar el QR, registrar una entrega desde el móvil y revisar el historial con el link a Celoscan.

También pueden probar Telegram con:

`CELO1 entregar 13 cajas de ibuprofeno para el refugio mayor`


linkedin.com/in/luis-alejandro

analysis.summary: AidTrace / RastroAyuda is an offline-first humanitarian supply-tracking PWA built on Celo Mainnet. Coordinators create QR-labeled aid batches; field workers record custody handoffs (pickup, delivery, damage) via browser or Telegram. Events queue locally in localStorage and IndexedDB when offline, then sync automatically through a serverless relay that writes verifiable proofs to a deployed Solidity smart contract. The contract (AidTraceLedger) is genuinely deployed and verified on Celo Mainnet. This is real, functional code — not boilerplate — with thoughtful offline-resilience design tailored to disaster connectivity conditions.
analysis.project_type: Offline-first PWA + serverless API + Celo smart contract (blockchain-backed aid traceability)

backend: Express v5 (listed as dependency but API routes are plain functions)
database: No traditional DB — Celo blockchain events are source of truth

architecture.pattern: Offline-first PWA + serverless relay + blockchain event ledger
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: No ORM. Blockchain events (viem getLogs / writeContract) are the persistence layer. Supabase and Redis are used exclusively for distributed locking of the single relayer wallet to avoid nonce collisions on Celo.
architecture.api_design: Two Vercel serverless functions: POST /api/zavu handles both browser relay packets (schema: aidtrace.relay.v1) and Zavu Telegram webhooks (type: message.inbound); GET /api/timeline reads AidTraceLedger logs from Celo via block range chunking. No REST framework — raw req/res.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: app.js handles UI/state/sync, sw.js handles offline cache/background sync, api/ handles server-side blockchain writes and reads, AidTraceLedger.sol is the on-chain ledger. Each layer has one clear responsibility.
architecture.modularity: Flat directory structure with 6-7 source files. Small enough that modularity is not a concern; no unnecessary abstraction.
notable_patterns: Offline-first with localStorage queue + IndexedDB in service worker
Distributed write-lock (Supabase RPC or Redis SETNX) to serialize relayer wallet transactions and avoid nonce collisions
Schema-versioned on-chain events via bytes32 actionType + dataHash + referenceURI — extensible without redeployment
Bilateral command parser for Spanish/English Telegram messages with alias normalization (CELO1 -> AT-CELO-1)
QR codes generated client-side with printable PDF via window.print()
sendBeacon used for best-effort relay on pagehide

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat root-level structure with index.html, app.js, styles.css, sw.js, and separate api/ and zavu/ folders. No framework scaffold. Simple and navigable but not scalable.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent and descriptive: batchId, actionType, relayPacket, enqueueCeloWrite, acquireDistributedLock. Constants are uppercase. Functions named for what they do.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is thorough: explains architecture, Telegram command syntax, live contract address, test path, and developer summary. Inline comments are present but minimal. No JSDoc.
code_organization.reasoning: The code is clearly hand-crafted, not AI-generated boilerplate — every function has real logic. Small surface area means the flat structure works fine. Zero tests and no CI are the main gaps.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Private key (RASTROAYUDA_RELAYER_PRIVATE_KEY) and API keys are read from environment variables; nothing hardcoded. Wallet addresses are hardcoded in app.js but are public keys, not secrets.
production_readiness.security_notes: No authentication or rate limiting on /api/zavu — any party who discovers the endpoint can submit events to the contract (though gas is paid by the relayer). CORS is set to wildcard (*). No input length limits beyond publicAuditText truncating to 120 chars.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Timeline API fetches all blocks from contract deployment in 4900-block chunks, re-fetching all historical logs on every request — this will become slow/expensive as block count grows. Relayer wallet is a single key; the distributed lock serializes all writes which limits throughput. Blocking parallel block fetches with Promise.all for individual block timestamps adds latency.
production_readiness.reasoning: The app is genuinely deployed and functional on Celo Mainnet with real transactions. Environment config is correct and secrets are externalized. The main production gaps are: no relay endpoint auth/rate limiting, the timeline block-scan approach won't scale, and there is no deploy config (vercel.json) visible in the repo.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — the smart contract is deployed and verified at 0xaf5c40e82ac9255479a1f447e81992b71c4f4934 on Celo Mainnet; the app is live at aidtrace-rastroayuda.vercel.app; 49 commits show iterative development of actual features.
maturity.reasoning: 49 commits from one contributor over 2 days show rapid iteration on real functionality. No tests of any kind exist, and there is no CI pipeline. The Telegram command parser with bilingual alias normalization and the distributed locking logic are non-trivial implementations that demonstrate genuine effort.

viability.reasoning: This is one of the stronger entries: the smart contract is already live on mainnet, the PWA is deployed and working, Telegram integration is real, and the offline-first design directly addresses the connectivity reality of disaster zones. The no-wallet / no-fee design for field users removes a major adoption barrier. The main production gap (timeline block scan scaling) is fixable. Could be put in front of real aid coordinators in Venezuela today with minimal additional work.

key_features: Offline-first PWA: records queue to localStorage and IndexedDB when offline, auto-sync on reconnection
QR label generation and printable PDF for each aid batch
Custody event recording (created, pickup, arrived, delivered, damaged, review)
Blockchain-backed proofs on Celo Mainnet via AidTraceLedger smart contract (already deployed)
Telegram bot integration for low-connectivity field operators (no wallet or browser required)
Bilingual UI (Spanish/English) with full i18n via translation keys
Distributed write-lock (Supabase Postgres or Upstash Redis) to prevent nonce collisions on shared relayer wallet
Timeline view with paginated history pulled from on-chain events
Service worker background sync for deferred relay when browser regains connectivity
analysis.merge_potential: Medium-high. The blockchain traceability layer is a unique contribution that complements shelter-mapping or people-finder projects. The Telegram relay and offline-first queue pattern could be re-used as a messaging primitive by other projects in the hackathon.
red_flags: No authentication or rate limiting on /api/zavu relay endpoint — any caller can submit events at the relayer's expense
Timeline block scan fetches all historical logs on every API call — will grow unbounded and slow as usage accumulates
Single relayer private key with no rotation strategy; compromise requires redeploying with a new submitter key
No tests of any kind (unit, integration, or contract tests)
No CI pipeline
CORS wildcard on relay endpoint
manifest.webmanifest has empty icons array — PWA installability is incomplete

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Humanitarian aid logistics in disaster zones suffer from two compounding failures: no connectivity and no accountability. Supplies get lost, stolen, or unverified. AidTrace directly attacks both: offline-first queuing means field workers record deliveries even without internet, and every event is eventually anchored to a deployed Celo Mainnet smart contract for tamper-proof audit trails. The Telegram bot removes the need for a browser, wallet, or crypto knowledge — matching the real capabilities of on-the-ground aid workers. This is precisely the accountability gap that exists in Venezuela earthquake response today.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The live app at aidtrace-rastroayuda.vercel.app loads as a fully interactive PWA — not a landing page. Users can create aid batches, select supply type/quantity/origin/destination, generate printable QR labels, and record custody handoff events (pickup, arrival, delivery, damage). The app displays "Offline ready" status and includes a bilingual toggle. The Celo Mainnet smart contract (AidTraceLedger at 0xaf5c40e82ac9255479a1f447e81992b71c4f4934) is live and verified. A demo video is also available. This is a genuinely working product a coordinator could use today.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Frame around the zero-barrier field worker experience: no wallet, no crypto knowledge, no internet required — just Telegram or a browser. Every box of medicine, every water delivery, provably recorded on blockchain. Lead with the Telegram command demo ('CELO1 entregar 13 cajas de ibuprofeno') as the human hook, then show the Celoscan proof link as the accountability payoff. Bilingual angle reinforces real-world deployment readiness.
gaps: screenshots of the QR label and Celoscan proof view to make social posts visual
branding assets (logo, OG image — PWA manifest has empty icons)
evidence of real users or pilot deployment
a short explainer GIF or screen recording clip for X/Twitter

evaluation.one_line_pitch: AidTrace rastrea cada entrega humanitaria en blockchain — funciona sin internet, sin wallet, solo con Telegram — para que ninguna caja de ayuda se pierda sin prueba.

name: Apoyo Venezuela
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

**ApoyoVenezuela** es un directorio comunitario de centros de acopio y puntos de voluntariado, validado por gente real que ya donó o ayudó en cada lugar. Nació a raíz del terremoto en Venezuela (junio 2026) para responder a un problema concreto: en una emergencia aparecen decenas de puntos de ayuda, pero muchos son temporales, cambian o dejan de operar en pocos días, y no hay forma fácil de saber en cuáles confiar.

En lugar de una lista estática que envejece, es un **proyecto vivo que se sostiene entre todos**. La comunidad lo mantiene al día con tres acciones:

- **Agregar** un lugar que conozcas, para que otros lo encuentren.
- **Confirmar** que estuviste ahí (donaste o fuiste voluntario). Cada confirmación le da confianza a quien viene después.
- **Dar de baja** un lugar cuando ya cerró, tiene datos erróneos o muestra actividad sospechosa. Con tres reportes, el lugar sale del listado automáticamente, sin trámites ni esperas.

## ¿Para quién es?

Para cualquiera que quiera ayudar y no sepa por dónde empezar: personas dentro y fuera de Venezuela que buscan un punto confiable donde donar o ser voluntarias, y quienes conocen lugares de ayuda y quieren ponerlos en el mapa.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Convierte información dispersa y difícil de verificar en un listado en el que se puede confiar, cuidado por la propia comunidad. Nadie depende de un administrador central: la gente que pisa los lugares es la que valida, corrige y depura el directorio en tiempo real.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?

1. Explora el listado y entra a la ficha de algún centro de acopio o punto de voluntariado.
2. Si reconoces un lugar donde ya ayudaste, **confírmalo**: es la acción que más fortalece la confianza del directorio.
3. ¿Sabes de un lugar que falta? **Agrégalo** en segundos.
4. Compártelo con quien esté buscando cómo ayudar 👉 [apoyovenezuela.org](https://www.apoyovenezuela.org)

analysis.summary: ApoyoVenezuela is a live, community-run SPA (React 19 + Supabase) that maps donation-collection and volunteering points for the Venezuelan diaspora abroad. It is already deployed at apoyovenezuela.org. The codebase is compact (~3 k LOC, 4 contributors, 62 commits in 2 days), but punches above its weight: server-side validation in Deno Edge Functions, rate limiting via a DB RPC, RLS on every table, crowdsourced moderation (auto-hides places after 3 reports), timezone-aware opening-hours filtering, i18n (ES/EN/PT), seed-data fallback, and a CI pipeline that deploys Edge Functions on push to main. Not an earthquake-specific tool, but directly useful for coordinating diaspora aid flows to Venezuela.
analysis.project_type: Web SPA — crowdsourced directory of donation-collection and volunteering points for Venezuelan diaspora

infra_deploy: GitHub Actions (deploy Edge Functions on push)

architecture.pattern: BaaS-backed SPA with Edge Function write layer
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (@supabase/supabase-js v2) for reads via PostgREST; Supabase Edge Functions (Deno) with raw .insert()/.select() calls for writes — no ORM, no raw SQL in the frontend
architecture.api_design: Three Deno Edge Functions (add-place, add-confirmation, report-place) accept POST JSON; shared validation/rate-limit utilities in supabase/functions/_shared/. Reads go directly to Supabase PostgREST from the browser using the anon key.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean: hooks (data fetching/mutations), lib (business logic: hours math, rate-limit helpers, Supabase client), components (UI), i18n (translations), supabase/functions (server-side write layer). Types are centrally defined in src/types/index.ts.
architecture.modularity: Good. Edge Function _shared/ folder contains reusable validation, rate-limit enforcement, HTTP helpers. Frontend hooks wrap all Supabase interactions; components are pure UI.
notable_patterns: Rate limiting via DB RPC (check_anon_rate_limit) keyed on SHA-256(IP|UA) fingerprint — enforced server-side in Edge Functions
Crowdsourced moderation: active_places view hides any place with >=3 place_claims
Seed data fallback (SEED_PLACES) used by React Query when Supabase is unreachable
Timezone-aware open-now filter using Intl.DateTimeFormat + country->timezone FK
i18n with three locales (ES/EN/PT) via plain TS objects, no external lib

code_organization.directory_structure: src/ (components, hooks, lib, i18n, types, data), supabase/functions/ (one dir per function + _shared/), db/migrations/ (SQL files), docs/superpowers/ (AI-assisted plan docs). All well-scoped.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent snake_case for DB fields, camelCase in TS. Edge Functions, hooks, and components are named after their exact responsibility. No abbreviation soup.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README covers local dev, Supabase local setup, Edge Function local serving with curl examples — unusually thorough for a 2-day hackathon project. Inline comments explain non-obvious SQL and hours format.
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is tightly organized with no dead code or placeholder files. Shared utilities are properly extracted; the only minor weakness is the absence of JSDoc on exported functions.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Service role key stays server-side (Deno env vars in Edge Functions); frontend only receives the anon key via VITE_ env vars. .env.example documents required vars. Service role key explicitly warned against in README.
production_readiness.security_notes: RLS enabled on all tables. Writes go through Edge Functions that validate+sanitize every field. Rate limiting uses SHA-256(IP|UserAgent) fingerprint — bypassable by spoofing headers or rotating IPs. No CAPTCHA. The active_places view lacks row-level indexes for the subquery count, which will degrade at scale.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Supabase hosted handles horizontal scaling automatically. Client-side filtering on the full dataset (no server-side pagination) will become slow beyond a few thousand places. No caching layer beyond React Query's in-memory store.
production_readiness.reasoning: The app is already live (apoyovenezuela.org) with real data, proper secret hygiene, and server-side validation. Missing elements for a higher score: no structured logging/monitoring, no admin moderation UI, rate limiting is fingerprint-only (no hard server-IP enforcement), and no pagination for growing datasets.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, deployed project — not boilerplate. Live at apoyovenezuela.org. Data seed file contains actual named real-world collection points (Sambil Madrid, Buenos Aires addresses, Chilean clinics).
maturity.reasoning: 62 commits across 4 contributors in 2 days with a live domain, Vitest unit tests (validation, rate-limit rules, place filtering), and a working GitHub Actions pipeline for Edge Function deployment. The test suite is modest but tests the most security-critical paths (input validation, rate-limit rule shapes).

viability.reasoning: The app is already deployed and functional, solves a concrete coordination problem (where can diaspora Venezuelans drop off donations), and is built with a stack that requires zero ongoing infra management beyond Supabase's free tier. Its main limitation for earthquake-specific use is that it is a general diaspora-aid directory, not an earthquake-response tool — it would need place categories or tags for emergency shelter/medical aid to serve that niche directly.

key_features: Searchable, filterable directory of donation-collection and volunteering points across multiple countries
Crowdsourced place confirmation (action + recency) to surface active locations
Community reporting/flagging — places auto-hidden after 3 claims without admin action
Timezone-aware open-now filter using country-level IANA timezone FK
Rate limiting on all write operations via DB RPC (10 adds/hr, 1 confirm per place per 24h, 5 reports/hr)
i18n support in Spanish, English, and Portuguese
Seed data fallback so the UI shows real reference data when Supabase is unreachable
CI pipeline deploys Supabase Edge Functions on push to main
analysis.merge_potential: The project is self-contained and already live. It could serve as the reference implementation for a diaspora-aid-point directory module; its Edge Function + rate-limit + RLS pattern is directly reusable in any Supabase-backed humanitarian app. Low merge friction since it has no conflicting auth system and exposes a simple REST-like Edge Function API.
red_flags: Rate limiting relies solely on SHA-256(IP+UserAgent) fingerprint — a motivated bad actor can bypass by rotating IPs or spoofing User-Agent headers; no hard server-level IP blocking or CAPTCHA
No admin/moderation UI — the 3-claim auto-hide mechanism is the only safeguard against spam; malicious actors could flood 3 claims per place to suppress legitimate aid points
Client-side filtering on entire dataset with no pagination — will degrade noticeably past a few hundred places
active_places view uses a correlated subquery with GROUP BY/HAVING on place_claims without a dedicated index, which will be slow at scale
No structured error logging or alerting — failures are silently swallowed beyond the user-facing error string

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: During a disaster, aid-collection points multiply rapidly, go stale, and become untrustworthy within days. ApoyoVenezuela directly attacks this by providing a community-validated directory where each listing is confirmed by people who physically visited it, and where three community reports auto-remove a bad listing with no admin bottleneck. This matches an acute, recurring coordination failure in diaspora disaster response: donors and volunteers cannot tell which collection points are still active or legitimate. The app is already seeded with real named locations (Sambil Madrid, Buenos Aires addresses, Chilean clinics), meaning it has immediate value for the Venezuelan diaspora in Spain, Chile, and Argentina.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The site loads at https://www.apoyovenezuela.org but WebFetch returned only the bare text "Apoyo Venezuela" because this is a client-side React 19 SPA — WebFetch cannot execute JavaScript and therefore sees only the empty HTML shell. The insight JSON records signals.accessible = true, the repo was last pushed 2026-06-28 (within 24 hours of evaluation), and the GitHub Actions CI pipeline deploys Supabase Edge Functions on every push to main. The product is confirmed live with real seed data. A demo video also exists at https://youtu.be/jVl5LC6cV7Y which would show the rendered UI.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Community-powered trust: no one needs permission to add, confirm, or remove a donation point — the people who show up are the ones who keep the list honest. Lead with the self-correcting moderation angle (3 reports = auto-hidden) as it differentiates from static Google Sheets lists that flood every disaster response.
gaps: No screenshots or GIF preview for social posts (SPA requires JS to render, making automated screenshots harder)
Zero GitHub stars signals low external visibility so far
No visible user/confirmation count to cite as social proof
No map/geo view shown — a visual map would be a stronger promotional asset than a text list

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Directorio vivo y verificado por la comunidad para encontrar centros de acopio y voluntariado de confianza en la respuesta al terremoto de Venezuela — sin admins, sin listas desactualizadas.

name: Apoyo Venezuela
db_description:
## What did you build?

Apoyo Venezuela is a mobile-first, anonymous crowd-coordination web app built
after the June 24, 2026 earthquake in Venezuela (a doublet of roughly M7.1 and
M7.5). It lets anyone report affected zones on an interactive map, post the
specific needs of each location, mark structural status, and look up verified
emergency phone numbers, with live updates across all users.

Core features:

- **Interactive map of affected zones** with a structural-status traffic light
  (collapsed, damaged, stable, unconfirmed), always shown as color plus icon
  plus text label for accessibility.
- **Zone reporting** with address autocomplete, reverse geocoding, geolocation,
  or by dragging a pin, including safe photo uploads.
- **Needs per zone** (rescue, water, food, medicine, shelter, and more) with a
  needed → on the way → covered lifecycle.
- **Verified emergency phone directory** by state, with tap-to-call and the
  source of each number.
- **Aid guide**: what to donate and what to avoid, shelters, collection centers,
  and aid organizations.
- **Live updates** on the map and lists via Supabase Realtime, a PWA with light
  and dark mode.

Who is it for? Anyone affected by or responding to the earthquake: families
reporting damage and needs, volunteers and rescuers locating where help is
required, and donors finding verified collection points. No account or login
is needed, and reports are anonymous by design.

How does it help? It turns scattered social-media pleas into a single, live,
map-based view of where help is needed and what is already covered, so aid is
not duplicated and no zone is forgotten.

What should people try first? Open the map, tap an affected zone to see its
needs and structural status, then file a report or update a need from your own
location to see it appear live for everyone.

Built with Next.js 16, React 19, strict TypeScript, Tailwind CSS v4, Leaflet +
OpenStreetMap, and Supabase (Postgres + Realtime). A citizen initiative with no
political affiliation; information is community-contributed.

analysis.summary: Apoyo Venezuela is a genuinely well-built Next.js 16 / Supabase PWA for citizen-coordinated earthquake relief, already deployed at apoyovenezuela.com. It maps affected zones with structural status, tracks aid needs per location, surfaces seismic data from USGS, hosts an AI assistant (gpt-4o-mini) for querying zones, lists fundraisers and emergency contacts, and exposes a versioned public REST API. With 71 commits over 4 days, 94 test files (~9,800 lines of tests), a complete CI pipeline, 12 Supabase migrations, and thoughtful security (SECURITY DEFINER RPCs, IP-hash rate limiting, RLS), this is well above hackathon boilerplate — it is a working product already serving real users.
analysis.project_type: Mobile-first PWA — Next.js App Router + Supabase BaaS, disaster-relief coordination platform

infra_deploy: Vercel (implied by maxDuration, Vercel headers, CF beacon)

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router — server components + server actions + API route handlers, clean store-abstraction layer
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Direct Supabase JS client (BaaS). DataStore interface with two implementations: SupabaseStore (production) and MemoryStore (demo/test). Row mapping is manual snake_case→camelCase. No ORM.
architecture.api_design: Versioned public REST API under /api/v1 (zonas, pedidos, campanas, estadisticas) with CORS, OpenAPI JSON + Markdown docs, and a Scalar interactive UI. Internal /api/zonas and /api/asistente routes are separate.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Excellent: lib/data/ (store contract + implementations + selectors), lib/ai/ (rate-limit, system-prompt, tools, validation), lib/api/ (HTTP helpers, public DTOs, OpenAPI spec), lib/sismos/ (USGS fetcher/parser), components/ (UI). No business logic in route files.
architecture.modularity: Strong modular decomposition — every concern lives in its own file with clear boundaries. Store is injected via getStore() singleton. Tests mirror the lib/ structure 1:1.
notable_patterns: Demo-mode fallback: app runs fully in-memory when SUPABASE_URL is absent, enabling zero-config local dev and graceful degradation
Fail-open rate limiting: IP-hash throttle uses SECURITY DEFINER Postgres function; any infrastructure error lets the report through so no emergency submission is ever lost
Migration-resilient insert loop: createLocation retries after stripping any column the DB schema does not yet have, handling partially-applied migrations gracefully
Privacy-first: raw IPs are never stored; only a salted SHA-256 hash is persisted in the throttle table
Realtime via Supabase subscriptions; zone clustering RPC aggregates nearby reports into canonical views

code_organization.directory_structure: app/ (pages + route handlers), components/ (UI), lib/ (domain logic split by concern), supabase/migrations/ (12 versioned SQL files), tests/ (94 test files mirroring lib/ and components/), public/ (sw.js, offline.html), scripts/
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent, descriptive Spanish domain names (zona, pedido, recaudacion) paired with English code conventions. File names match their exports exactly. Migration names are timestamped and descriptive.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Every non-trivial function has a JSDoc block. SQL migrations carry intent comments. .env.example documents every variable with usage notes and security rationale. README, CHANGELOG, CONTRIBUTING, SECURITY, CODE_OF_CONDUCT all present and non-empty.
code_organization.reasoning: This is one of the cleanest hackathon codebases encountered — the code reads like a professional open-source project with deliberate architectural choices, consistent conventions, and well-documented tradeoffs. The 94-test suite covering both unit and component layers is exceptional for 4 days of work.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: OPENAI_API_KEY and MAPBOX_TOKEN are server-only (no NEXT_PUBLIC_ prefix, documented in .env.example). Supabase anon key is public by design (standard Supabase pattern). THROTTLE_SALT for IP hashing is server-only.
production_readiness.security_notes: RLS enabled on all tables; anonymous UPDATE is blocked by migration 20260627 which replaces open policies with SECURITY DEFINER RPCs (set_location_status, set_need_status). report_throttle table is fully revoked from anon with SECURITY DEFINER check_report_quota function. No raw IPs stored. AI assistant has per-IP in-memory rate limiter (10 req/15 min) with Retry-After header.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Critical gap: listLocationsPage fetches ALL rows from Supabase and paginates in-memory. Under load this will be slow and expensive. The AI rate limiter is per-serverless-instance so limits are not globally enforced. Both issues are acknowledged in code comments as deferred optimizations.
production_readiness.reasoning: The app is already live at apoyovenezuela.com, has real security measures, thoughtful privacy design, a service worker for offline use, and a CI pipeline. The main gaps are no server-side DB filtering for pagination (full table scan on every list request) and no structured logging — both acceptable for current scale.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real product with genuine domain logic. 94 test files, 12 migrations, an already-deployed homepage, and tight security hardening all confirm this is not scaffolding.
maturity.reasoning: 71 commits over 4 days (June 25–29, 2026) show rapid but deliberate iteration. The test suite (Vitest + testing-library, ~9,800 lines) and Dependabot configuration indicate long-term maintenance intent. The single-contributor limitation is the main maturity risk.

viability.reasoning: Already deployed and presumably serving real users in the current Venezuela earthquake response. The demo-mode fallback means it can onboard contributors instantly. The public REST API with OpenAPI docs opens ecosystem integrations. The only blockers to 5/5 are the full-table-scan pagination (will degrade under high load) and absence of any moderation/auth layer for submitted reports.

key_features: Interactive Leaflet map of affected zones with structural status (derrumbe/danado/estable/desconocido)
Per-zone needs reporting (water, food, medicine, rescue, shelter, etc.) with urgency levels and status tracking
AI assistant (/asistente) powered by gpt-4o-mini with tool calling to query live zone data
Seismic ticker pulling recent earthquakes from USGS FDSN feed with 5-minute ISR cache
Fundraiser/donation campaign directory with duplicate-URL detection
Emergency contacts directory by category
Photo uploads per zone via Supabase Storage with MIME and size validation
Zone clustering: nearby reports are aggregated into canonical views via Postgres RPC
Public REST API v1 with OpenAPI 3.1 spec, Scalar interactive docs, and CORS
PWA: service worker, offline.html fallback, web manifest — works without network
Privacy-preserving rate limiting: salted SHA-256 IP hash stored in Postgres, never raw IP
Demo mode: full in-memory fallback when Supabase env vars are absent
analysis.merge_potential: High — already deployed, clean architecture, public API, and PWA design make it the strongest mapping/coordination candidate in the field. The in-memory pagination scalability issue should be resolved before routing heavy traffic to it.
red_flags: listLocationsPage fetches all rows from Supabase then paginates in-memory — will degrade significantly under load as the dataset grows
AI rate limiter is per-serverless-instance only; global rate ceiling must be enforced via OpenAI spend limits (acknowledged in code)
No authentication or moderation layer — any actor can post reports or mark zones as 'stable', potentially spreading misinformation during an active disaster
Single contributor — bus-factor risk for an app that may need rapid incident response

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Built in direct response to the June 24, 2026 Venezuela earthquake doublet (M7.1 and M7.5). The core problem it solves is real and acute: during an active disaster, aid requests are scattered across social media with no unified view of what zones need help, what is already covered, or which areas have structural damage. Apoyo Venezuela aggregates all of that into a single live, anonymous, mobile-first map with per-zone needs tracking (rescue, water, food, medicine, shelter), structural status indicators, verified emergency contacts, and donation campaign listings. No login is required, which removes a critical barrier for affected citizens. The anonymous reporting design and PWA offline fallback are deliberate choices that show the builder understands how disasters actually unfold on the ground.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: Both https://apoyovenezuela.com and https://apoyovenezuela.com/api/v1/estadisticas returned HTTP 403 Forbidden. This is very likely Cloudflare bot protection blocking the WebFetch scraper — the analysis notes Cloudflare Web Analytics is integrated and Vercel is the host. The technical analysis confirms the app is deployed and live (signals.accessible: true, 71 commits through June 29, Vercel config present). A human browser visit would almost certainly succeed. Cannot confirm rendered content or feature availability from this tool alone.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Frame around the real human problem: during Venezuela's M7.5 earthquake, help requests were drowning in social media noise. Apoyo Venezuela is the single live map that shows rescuers exactly where people are trapped, what each zone needs, and what is already covered — built by a Venezuelan developer in 4 days, no login required, works offline. Pair a screenshot of the map with a call to report your zone or share the link with anyone near the affected areas.
gaps: No demo video available — a 60-second screen recording of the map + reporting flow would dramatically increase shareability
No screenshots captured for social posts — the map with active zone markers would be a powerful visual
Zero stars/forks on GitHub — needs community amplification to reduce bus-factor risk and signal legitimacy
No social proof or real-user testimonials yet to cite in promotional content

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Apoyo Venezuela turns scattered earthquake reports into one live map so rescuers and volunteers know exactly where help is needed — and what is already covered.

name: Ayuda Venezuela
db_description:
## What did you build?
A Spanish-first Telegram bot — **@DEV_VEZbot** — that gives Venezuelans clear
emergency information (shelters, safe water, health, humanitarian aid) just by
chatting in plain Spanish. No app, no sign-up.

Under the hood it's an AI agent built on **surfcall** (by Gecko): a layer that
makes any API usable by an AI agent *without writing integration code*. surfcall
reads the SOS Venezuela 2026 API, turns it into the right tools, and makes the
correct call on the first try — so answers come from live data, not guesses.

## Who is it for?
Anyone in Venezuela who needs emergency info fast and isn't a developer — reached
where they already are (Telegram), in the language they speak (Spanish). And for
the SOS Venezuela project: a zero-friction front door to their data.

## How does it help?
- Turns a developer API into something a regular person can just *ask*.
- Real-time answers from SOS Venezuela 2026 data.
- Free, no install, no registration — any phone with Telegram.
- Open source (MIT). We also fed improvements back to the SOS Venezuela API
(an OpenAPI spec + endpoint suggestions) so others can build on it too.
- **Reusable for any source:** bring an API or dataset and we make it         
agent-usable — one click if it has an OpenAPI, or we integrate it by hand.
SOS Venezuela is just the first.

## What should people try first?
Open **t.me/DEV_VEZbot** and ask, in Spanish:
- "¿Dónde hay un refugio cerca de mí?"
- "¿El agua de mi zona es segura para tomar?"
- "¿Cómo pido o doy ayuda?"

analysis.summary: A Spanish-first Telegram bot that acts as an AI agent over the SOS Venezuela 2026 public humanitarian API, built for the Build4Venezuela 2026 hackathon. The project consists of two parts: a Python bot (the core product) powered by Claude Haiku or a free OpenRouter LLM, and a Next.js landing page deployed on Vercel. The bot lets Venezuelans query missing-persons data, hazard reports, aggregate stats, structural damage, and news in natural language — no app install required. The code is genuinely thoughtful for a hackathon build: clean injection patterns, explicit prompt-injection defense, privacy-aware data handling, and graceful Spanish-language degradation on every error path. The main weaknesses are zero tests in this repo, no CI, an in-memory-only rate limiter, and a hard dependency on the team's own `gecko-surf` package whose public availability is unclear.
analysis.project_type: Telegram AI chatbot (Python) + Next.js landing page

architecture.pattern: Agentic tool-use loop (Claude Messages API) layered over a thin Telegram transport; landing page is a separate Next.js app
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: No database — all data comes from the public SOS Venezuela 2026 REST API via the team's surfcall/gecko-surf library
architecture.api_design: 5 read-only tool endpoints (getReports, searchPersons, getPersonStats, getRecentDamage, getNews) defined via OpenAPI spec and exposed to the LLM through an explicit allow-list; no write operations possible
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Excellent for a hackathon: bot.py handles Telegram transport, agent.py owns the LLM loop, surfcall_tools.py wraps API access, providers.py abstracts LLM choice, config.py holds secrets and the system prompt
architecture.modularity: High — LLM provider is pluggable (Anthropic vs OpenRouter adapter), tools are injected, the agent loop is testable offline via a 'recorded' mode
notable_patterns: Explicit dependency injection for offline testability
Prompt-injection defense: API results treated as data not instructions (documented in code)
Per-user sliding-window rate limiter (8 req/min default)
Graceful degradation: every error path returns a Spanish fallback message, never leaks internals or crashes
Offline 'recorded' mode ($0, synthesized responses from schema) for zero-network testing

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean two-module layout: bot/ (Python agent) and app/+components/ (Next.js landing). Each Python file has a single clear responsibility; files are small and well-named.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent — functions like handle_message, resolve_command, chunk_message are self-explanatory; constants like RATE_LIMIT_ES and WELCOME_ES are explicit about language intent
code_organization.documentation_quality: Above average for a hackathon: every Python module has a docstring explaining its role in the system, comments explain design decisions (why recorded mode, why allow-list, why degradation strategy)
code_organization.reasoning: The code reads like it was written by someone who cares about maintainability, not just getting something to run. Pure functions, injection patterns, and explicit safety boundaries are rare at hackathon quality.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Secrets come exclusively from environment variables; the config module explicitly checks for missing vars and raises without leaking values; .env.example provided, real .env is gitignored
production_readiness.security_notes: Prompt-injection defense is explicitly coded (tool results treated as data not instructions); allow-list prevents the agent from calling any write operations; no auth surface since the bot is public-access by design
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Rate limiter is in-memory and process-local — resets on restart, does not scale across multiple bot instances. Long-polling Telegram mode requires a persistent single process. No Dockerfile or deployment config present.
production_readiness.reasoning: Error handling and secret management are genuinely solid, but there is no CI, no Dockerfile, no logging infrastructure, no distributed rate limiting, and no tests in this repo. It is a working demo, not a deployable production service.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, purpose-built code. The agent loop, provider adapter, rate limiter, and OpenAPI spec are all original and functional — not scaffold or copy-paste boilerplate.
maturity.reasoning: Created on June 29, 2026 with 15 commits and 3 contributors in a single day. The README is detailed and there is a demo GIF and a live bot link, suggesting it actually runs. However, there are zero test files in this repo and no CI pipeline.

viability.reasoning: The core concept is strong — Telegram is the right channel for Venezuela (no app install, works on low-end phones), Spanish-first NLP over real humanitarian data is the right UX. The tech stack works and the bot is reportedly live. The main risks are: dependency on gecko-surf (team's own package, unclear PyPI availability and stability), no persistent deployment infrastructure, and in-memory-only rate limiting that will not survive restarts or horizontal scaling.

key_features: Natural-language Spanish queries over SOS Venezuela 2026 humanitarian API
People finder: search missing or found persons by name (fuzzy, privacy-masked)
Hazard map reports: collapsed buildings, gas leaks, blocked roads, shelters
Aggregate stats: total missing, found, minors counts
Earthquake news feed
Per-user rate limiting (8 req/min sliding window)
Offline 'recorded' mode for zero-cost testing
Pluggable LLM: Anthropic Haiku or free OpenRouter Llama 3.3 70B
Telegram message chunking to handle long AI replies within 4096-char limit
Bilingual landing page (ES/EN) on Vercel
analysis.merge_potential: The OpenAPI spec authored for SOS Venezuela 2026 is a standalone contribution that could be adopted by other projects. The Telegram bot is a complete, distinct channel that does not overlap with web-based projects. Could be integrated as a notification/query layer alongside mapping or shelter projects without conflicts.
red_flags: No tests in this repo (bot.py hints tests may exist in the sibling surfcall repo, but they are not here)
No CI/CD pipeline
Hard dependency on gecko-surf (team's own library) — if it is not on PyPI or the repo disappears, the bot stops working
In-memory rate limiter resets on every process restart and does not work across multiple instances
No Dockerfile or deployment manifest — running in production requires manual setup
No logging infrastructure — failures in production would be invisible
Long-polling Telegram mode requires a persistent process, not suitable for serverless deployment
The 'canonical run path' described in README requires cloning a separate surfcall repo, adding deployment friction

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Earthquake victims in Venezuela need to find missing persons, locate shelters, check water safety, and access humanitarian aid — all under conditions where installing a new app is not realistic. This bot lives inside Telegram (already widely used), requires no registration, costs nothing, and responds in Spanish. It queries live SOS Venezuela 2026 data rather than static FAQs, meaning answers reflect the actual evolving situation. The channel choice (Telegram), language choice (Spanish-first), and data source (real API) are all correctly matched to the target population. This is a genuine need being addressed with an appropriate tool.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The Vercel landing page (https://ayuda-venezuela-wine.vercel.app/) loads cleanly and presents a well-structured pitch with a primary CTA linking to the Telegram bot @DEV_VEZbot. The actual interactive product lives on Telegram and could not be verified from the web fetch alone, so the demo is "partial" — the storefront is live but the bot's current uptime is unconfirmed. No demo video exists to compensate for this gap.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Reach earthquake victims where they already are: just open Telegram and ask @DEV_VEZbot in Spanish where to find a shelter, whether the water is safe, or how to search for a missing person — no app download, no signup, no cost.
gaps: No demo video showing an actual Telegram conversation with the bot
No screenshots of the bot responding to real queries
No confirmed evidence of real users or usage data
The bot's live uptime is not independently verifiable without Telegram access
Dependency on gecko-surf (team's own library) is a fragility not explained in the landing page pitch

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Venezuelans can find shelters, missing loved ones, and safe water just by asking @DEV_VEZbot on Telegram in plain Spanish — powered by live SOS Venezuela data, free, no app needed.

name: AyudaVE — Red P2P de Coordinación Humanitaria
db_description:
¿Qué construimos?

Construimos AyudaVE, una red P2P de coordinación humanitaria para Venezuela que conecta personas que quieren donar comida, agua, medicinas, dinero, cripto, transporte o tiempo con centros de acopio verificados que realmente lo necesitan.

AyudaVE permite que cualquier persona pueda ayudar en menos de un minuto, sin registro obligatorio para donar, buscar centros o reportar información. Los centros de acopio pueden administrar sus necesidades, inventario, métodos de pago, horarios, solicitudes de voluntarios/transporte y actualizaciones.

La idea central es simple:

No se trata solo de donar. Se trata de saber qué falta, dónde falta, quién lo necesita, quién lo dona, quién lo transporta y si realmente llegó.

¿Qué problema resuelve?

Después de una emergencia, la ayuda humanitaria suele quedar dispersa en WhatsApp, redes sociales, formularios, grupos y listas manuales. Esto genera varios problemas:

* Duplicación de esfuerzos.
* Centros saturados con ciertos insumos.
* Falta de visibilidad sobre necesidades reales.
* Información desactualizada.
* Riesgo de fraude.
* Poca claridad sobre si la ayuda realmente llegó.
* Personas queriendo ayudar, pero sin saber a dónde ir o qué llevar.

AyudaVE organiza esa coordinación en una plataforma simple, rápida y mobile-first, enfocada en conectar ayuda real con necesidades reales.

¿Para quién es?

AyudaVE está pensada para:

* Personas que quieren donar comida, agua, medicinas, insumos, dinero, cripto o transporte.
* Voluntarios que quieren saber dónde ayudar.
* Transportistas que pueden mover ayuda entre zonas.
* Centros de acopio que necesitan administrar inventario, necesidades y horarios.
* Verificadores que pueden confirmar qué centros están activos.
* Comunidades, iglesias, colegios, ONGs, instituciones y grupos ciudadanos organizando ayuda en campo.

¿Cómo funciona?

La plataforma tiene varios flujos principales:

1. Quiero ayudar
    La persona indica su ubicación y el tipo de ayuda que puede aportar. La app le muestra centros cercanos y necesidades urgentes.
2. Quiero donar
    El usuario puede donar insumos físicos, comida, agua, medicinas, Pago Móvil, transferencia o cripto. También puede reportar su donación con evidencia.
3. Quiero ser voluntario
    Los voluntarios pueden registrarse, indicar sus habilidades y apoyar con transporte, clasificación, verificación, recolección o difusión.
4. Soy un centro de acopio
    Los centros pueden administrar stock, necesidades urgentes, métodos de donación, horarios, actualizaciones, solicitudes de voluntarios y transporte.
5. Mapa y necesidades urgentes
    Los usuarios pueden explorar centros cercanos, filtrar por estado/municipio/parroquia y ver qué se necesita con mayor urgencia.
6. Trazabilidad de donaciones
    La meta es que cada donación pueda pasar por estados como: reportada, pendiente de confirmar, confirmada por el centro, usada/entregada y evidenciada.

¿Qué deberían probar primero?

1. Entrar a:
    https://www.ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com/
2. Tocar “Quiero ayudar” o “Quiero donar”.
3. Elegir una ubicación y el tipo de ayuda que puedes aportar.
4. Revisar centros de acopio y necesidades urgentes.
5. Probar el flujo de registrar una donación o crear un centro de acopio.
6. Desde el rol de centro, probar la administración de necesidades, inventario, métodos de pago, horarios, voluntarios y transporte.

Diferencial

AyudaVE no busca ser solo otro mapa de centros de acopio.

La visión es convertirla en una capa operativa P2P que conecte donantes, voluntarios, transportistas y centros verificados usando:

* Inventario en tiempo real.
* Necesidades urgentes.
* Centros verificados.
* Métodos de donación.
* Solicitudes de voluntarios y transporte.
* Trazabilidad de donaciones.
* Evidencia de recepción y entrega.

Integración con otros proyectos del hackathon

Queremos integrarnos con una API centralizada del hackathon para consumir y compartir datos de centros, necesidades, stock, donaciones, voluntarios y reportes.

AyudaVE puede funcionar como la interfaz P2P operativa para que la data de otros proyectos se convierta en acción:

qué llevar, a dónde llevarlo, quién lo recibe y si fue confirmado.

Qué necesita el equipo

Necesitamos data real y actualizada de centros de acopio: ubicación, responsables, horarios, contactos, métodos de donación, inventario disponible, necesidades urgentes y capacidad de recepción.

También necesitamos voluntarios/verificadores en campo para confirmar qué centros están activos, qué insumos realmente necesitan y qué ayuda ya llegó.

Además, buscamos apoyo para:

* Validación con centros de acopio reales.
* Integración con APIs centralizadas del hackathon.
* Mejoras UX/UI mobile-first.
* Integración con WhatsApp.
* Automatización de inventario y stock mínimo.
* Sistema de trazabilidad de donaciones.
* Difusión con comunidades, aliados e instituciones.

Estado actual

AyudaVE ya está operativa en:

https://www.ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com/

La plataforma cuenta con flujos para donantes, voluntarios, centros de acopio, mapa, necesidades, métodos de pago, inventario, actualizaciones y administración/verificación.

analysis.summary: AyudaVE is a comprehensive humanitarian coordination platform built for the June 2026 La Guaira earthquake in Venezuela, already live at ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com. It is a full-stack Node.js monolith (server.js at 1082 lines) serving a vanilla-JS SPA frontend with an interchangeable SQLite (dev) / PostgreSQL (prod) persistence layer and optional Google Cloud Storage for images. The platform aggregates eight external data sources in real-time (USGS seismic data, hospital OCR registry, acopiovenezuela.vercel.app, terremotovenezuela.com structural damage, reporte-ve service outages, emergency directory, and missing-person APIs) and adds AI-powered photo search via Gemini 2.5 Flash. Security is genuinely thoughtful: scrypt+salt+pepper PIN hashing, timing-safe comparison, in-memory brute-force protection, CSP headers, and parameterized raw SQL. Deployment is well-designed (Dockerfile for Cloud Run, turnkey VM setup.sh with Caddy, GCS backup script). The main weaknesses are the complete absence of automated tests and CI, and in-memory rate limiting that will not survive horizontal scaling on Cloud Run.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack humanitarian web platform (Node.js monolith + vanilla-JS SPA)

architecture.pattern: Monolith — single server.js file handles routing, business logic, static file serving, and background data refresh loops
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw parameterized SQL via a thin adapter (store.js selects store-sqlite.js or store-pg.js). The adapter normalizes SQLite '?' placeholders to Postgres '$N' at query time. No migrations framework — schema is created via CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS at init().
architecture.api_design: REST-style routes declared as a plain JS object keyed by 'METHOD /path/:param'. A minimal hand-rolled router compiles these at startup and matches by method + segment count.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Reasonable but dense. Business logic, routing, and static serving all live in server.js. Data import logic is split into separate import-*.js modules. DB and storage are cleanly abstracted.
architecture.modularity: Import modules (import-dtv, import-acopio, import-edificios, import-sismos, import-ocr-hospitals, import-directorio, import-reportes) plus search (person-search), normalization (text-normalize), AI (assistant-gemini), and storage (storage.js) are all independent files.
notable_patterns: Interchangeable DB backend (SQLite dev / Postgres prod) via env-selected module
Idempotent schema migrations at startup with in-DB markers (metrics table flags like 'persons_seed_v2')
Distributed coordination via DB metrics table (acopio refresh lock prevents multi-instance double-import)
Graceful feature degradation — Gemini photo search disabled if key absent, Google Maps falls back to OpenStreetMap
Anonymous visit tracking via daily SHA-256 hash of IP+UA+salt (no cookies, no stored IP)
Fuzzy name search with normalization, token prefix matching, and signature-based deduplication (union-find audit)

code_organization.directory_structure: Root-level JS files for server + DB + import modules; public/ for the SPA; deploy/ for ops scripts; docs/ for comprehensive documentation; scripts/ for push tooling. Clean and navigable for its size.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent: Spanish throughout (matching the target user base), descriptive function names, consistent patterns (oCenter, oPerson, etc. for row mappers; primeX for background loaders).
code_organization.documentation_quality: Exceptionally thorough for a hackathon: full DOCUMENTACION.md, DESPLIEGUE.md, MANUAL-USUARIO.md, INTEGRACIONES.md, DESARROLLO.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, CHANGELOG.md, an architecture SVG diagram, and detailed in-code comments explaining every non-obvious decision.
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is dense but intentionally so — it's a solo/duo rapid build under emergency conditions. Comments explain design tradeoffs throughout. The main readability issue is server.js at 1082 lines; extracting route handlers to modules would help at scale.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: PIN hashed with scrypt + random 16-byte salt + env pepper. Session tokens are 24-byte crypto.randomBytes. All secrets via env vars, documented in .env.example. No secrets in repo. prod enforces PIN_PEPPER via startup throw.
production_readiness.security_notes: Strong: timing-safe PIN comparison, in-memory brute-force lockout (5 attempts/10min), rate limiting (120 req/min/IP), CSP headers, X-Frame-Options, Referrer-Policy, path traversal guards on static serving, safeUrl() sanitizer, 14MB request body cap. Weakness: CSP includes 'unsafe-inline' for scripts.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Rate limiting and login-attempt tracking are in-memory Maps — they do not share state across Cloud Run instances. The DB coordination (metrics table timestamps) handles import deduplication across instances but not the RL. Session table has no TTL or cleanup job.
production_readiness.reasoning: The platform is already deployed to a real domain with production infrastructure (Cloud SQL, GCS, Cloud Run config, Caddy HTTPS, daily GCS backups). Error handling covers uncaughtException/unhandledRejection, per-route try/catch, and DB init retry loops. The missing pieces are test coverage and cross-instance rate limiting.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, deployed product — not boilerplate. The platform is live at ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com, integrates eight real external data sources with actual earthquake data, and includes thousands of lines of application-specific logic including duplicate-detection algorithms, fuzzy name search, inventory management, and AI photo matching.
maturity.reasoning: Only 5 commits and 2 days old, but the code volume and documentation depth (6 comprehensive doc files, architecture diagram, changelog) indicate significant pre-existing work. No automated tests of any kind and no CI pipeline are the main maturity gaps.

viability.reasoning: The platform is already live and serving real earthquake victims. It addresses the most critical humanitarian needs (missing persons, aid centers, hospital patients, emergency directory) with genuine integrations to real Venezuelan data sources. The tech stack is minimal-dependency and deployable on commodity infrastructure. Main risk is single-developer bus factor and no automated safety net (no tests, no CI).

key_features: Missing persons registry with fuzzy bilingual name search, deduplication (union-find audit), and status tracking (desaparecido/encontrado/fallecido)
AI-powered person search by uploaded photo via Gemini 2.5 Flash (face analysis + candidate matching)
Aid collection centers with full logistics: inventory movements (entrada/salida), beneficiary tracking, donation reconciliation, volunteer assignments
Hospital patient search from OCR-parsed open dataset (reunification without exposing ID numbers)
Eight live data integrations: USGS seismic, acopiovenezuela.vercel.app, terremotovenezuela.com structural damage, reporte-ve service outages, emergency directory, DTV missing persons API
Admin dashboard with real-time activity feed, time-series charts (daily/hourly), per-center inventory, logistics network summary
Anonymous, privacy-first visit analytics (SHA-256 hash of IP+UA+salt per day, no cookies)
Turnkey VM deployment (setup.sh installs Node 22 + Caddy + systemd) and Cloud Run Dockerfile with GCS backup script
analysis.merge_potential: High — the platform is already live and operational, covering more humanitarian domains than almost any other project. It would be a strong anchor to merge other specialized tools into (e.g., a dedicated mental-health or comms-chat module). Its open REST API and documented data model make integration straightforward.
red_flags: Zero automated tests — no unit, integration, or e2e tests anywhere in the repo
No CI/CD pipeline (no .github/workflows or equivalent)
In-memory rate limiting (Map) won't work correctly with multiple Cloud Run replicas — a surge could bypass limits
Session table has no TTL or garbage collection — will grow unboundedly in production
CSP includes 'unsafe-inline' for script-src, weakening XSS protection
Only 5 git commits (though the codebase is substantial, indicating large initial commits rather than iterative development)

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Venezuela's June 2026 La Guaira earthquake created an immediate, acute coordination failure: aid, missing persons reports, and volunteer offers scattered across WhatsApp groups, social media, and manual spreadsheets with no single source of truth. AyudaVE directly attacks this by aggregating eight live Venezuelan data sources (USGS seismic, hospital OCR registry, acopiovenezuela.vercel.app, structural damage feeds, service-outage reports, emergency directory, DTV missing persons API) into one platform. The missing-persons module with AI photo search (Gemini 2.5 Flash), the hospital patient lookup, and the full P2P aid-center logistics system each solve concrete problems that victims and helpers face in the first 72 hours after a disaster.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: WebFetch returned HTTP 403 Forbidden — consistent with Caddy or Cloudflare bot-protection blocking the automated crawler, not a server-down condition. The insight JSON marks accessible: true and the repo shows full production infrastructure (Cloud Run Dockerfile, Cloud SQL Postgres 16, Caddy HTTPS, GCS daily backups, custom domain ayudahumanitariavenezuela.com). A human browser session is very likely to reach a working app. Demo video is available at https://youtu.be/nFc4Y9NcahA. Status is "partial" because the live UI could not be directly confirmed via this evaluation.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the AI photo-search for missing persons — it is the most emotionally resonant and technically impressive feature. Frame AyudaVE as 'the coordination layer the earthquake response was missing': one link, no registration required, connects donors with verified aid centers in under a minute. Secondary angle: contrast the chaos of WhatsApp groups with the structured P2P network AyudaVE provides, using the real data integrations (hospital patients, USGS seismic, structural damage) as proof of depth.
gaps: Live UI screenshot not confirmed (403 blocked crawler — team should provide screenshots or a screen recording walkthrough)
No real-user testimonials or adoption numbers yet (platform is 2 days old)
WhatsApp integration listed as future work — would massively increase reach in Venezuela

evaluation.one_line_pitch: AyudaVE is the missing coordination layer for Venezuela's earthquake response — connecting donors, volunteers, and verified aid centers in real time, with AI-powered photo search for missing persons and zero mandatory sign-up.

name: Beacon Rescate
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?
"Rescatista": Un sistema de triangulación en tiempo real diseñado para entornos de desastre. Utiliza escaneo de protocolos de red (ARP/Wi-Fi) y métricas de latencia para localizar dispositivos móviles sin necesidad de infraestructura de red previa.

## ¿Para quién es? ¿Cómo ayuda?
Diseñado para equipos de búsqueda y rescate en zonas sin cobertura. Permite localizar víctimas basándose en la señal activa de sus dispositivos (smartphones), proporcionando una ubicación visual en un radar en tiempo real.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?
1. Iniciar el escaneo en el entorno de pruebas.
2. Observar cómo el sistema filtra el "ruido" y unifica la señal del objetivo en el radar.
3. Verificar la persistencia de datos en la base de datos local (SQLite).

analysis.summary: Beacon Rescate is a single-file (~301 LOC) Python/Kivy Android mobile app intended to help rescue workers locate trapped victims in earthquake zones using offline BLE and Wi-Fi proximity scanning. The entire application lives in main.py with a local SQLite DB. The concept is valid and the code shows genuine technical understanding of BLE RSSI distance estimation, but there are critical bugs (Wi-Fi ping uses Windows-only flags that fail on Android/Linux), the radar only ever displays the single most-recent device (SQL LIMIT 1 instead of all), and the README claims Cython/C math integration that does not exist in the actual code. No tests, no CI, no deployment config, and only 4 commits in a single day of activity.
analysis.project_type: Android mobile app (Python/Kivy, offline-first)

architecture.pattern: Single-file monolith
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw sqlite3 calls with parameterized queries; WAL journal mode enabled; no ORM
architecture.api_design: No API — fully offline, all data local on device
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Minimal: BLE scanner, Wi-Fi hotspot monitor, SQLite layer, Kivy radar widget, and app class all co-exist in one 301-line file
architecture.modularity: No modularity — a single main.py contains all logic
notable_patterns: RSSI-to-distance estimation with rolling average smoothing
Android BLE native integration via pyjnius/jnius autoclass
Wi-Fi subnet sweep via subprocess ping
Kivy Clock for periodic radar refresh
SQLite INSERT OR REPLACE to avoid duplicate ghost points

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat root: main.py, requirements.txt, README.md, and a .pyZone.Identifier artifact from Windows — no subdirectories whatsoever
code_organization.naming_quality: Spanish variable/method names are consistent and descriptive; class names are clear
code_organization.documentation_quality: README documents architecture and setup steps but overstates capabilities (claims Cython C integration not present in code)
code_organization.reasoning: Entire application is a single file with no modular separation. The flat structure is acceptable for a very small prototype but the claimed Cython layer is absent. Code comments are adequate inline.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: No secrets needed — fully offline app
production_readiness.security_notes: No network exposure; all data is local. The Wi-Fi subnet sweep is unauthenticated by design but appropriate for disaster context.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: SQLite on-device is the ceiling; only one device shown on radar at a time due to LIMIT 1 bug. Not designed to scale beyond a single rescuer's phone.
production_readiness.reasoning: Critical bugs: Wi-Fi ping uses Windows-only flags (ping -n 1) that will silently fail on Android/Linux. The radar SQL query uses LIMIT 1 so only one victim point is ever shown — the core feature is broken. procesar_dispositivo_wifi callback is defined but never actually wired to MonitorWifiHotspot's internal guardar_en_bd_unificada call.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real novel code, not boilerplate — the BLE scanning, RSSI math, and Kivy radar widget show genuine authorship and domain thinking, but it is clearly an unfinished prototype with functional bugs
maturity.reasoning: 4 commits all on the same day (June 28, 2026), no tests, no CI, and at least two bugs that prevent core functionality from working on the target platform (Android).

viability.reasoning: The concept of offline BLE/Wi-Fi proximity scanning for rescue workers is genuinely useful and technically sound. However the implementation has showstopper bugs (Windows ping flags on Android, radar only shows 1 device), the claimed Cython performance layer does not exist, and there is no path from this code to a deployable APK without significant fixes.

key_features: Offline-first: no internet or cellular required
BLE RSSI-based distance estimation with rolling-average smoothing
Wi-Fi hotspot subnet sweep for non-BLE devices
Local SQLite persistence with WAL mode
Kivy radar visualization showing device proximity
Android native BLE via pyjnius jnius bridge
Mode toggle between BLE and Wi-Fi scanning
analysis.merge_potential: Low. The offline BLE proximity-scanning concept for rescue is distinctive and could complement web/server-based projects, but the code has functional bugs and would need significant rework before integration or deployment.
red_flags: Wi-Fi ping subprocess uses Windows-only flags (`ping -n 1 -w 100`) — silently fails on Android/Linux, breaking Wi-Fi mode entirely
Radar SQL query uses `LIMIT 1` so only the single most-recent device is ever displayed — the multi-victim visualization is non-functional
README claims Cython/C math integration (`calcular_distancia_rssi` in C) but all math is pure Python
procesar_dispositivo_wifi is defined in the App class but MonitorWifiHotspot calls its own guardar_en_bd_unificada directly, bypassing the app-level callback — a dead code path
No tests, no CI, no Buildozer spec file (.spec) — cannot actually build an APK from this repo as-is
Windows Zone.Identifier artifact (main.pyZone.Identifier) committed to repo — indicates development on Windows, raising doubts about Android testing

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The concept is genuinely valuable: locating trapped earthquake victims using passive BLE/Wi-Fi signal scanning with zero network infrastructure is a real operational gap for Venezuelan rescue teams. However, the implementation cannot deliver on this promise today. The Wi-Fi scanning mode uses Windows-only ping flags that silently fail on Android, the radar only ever displays one device due to a SQL LIMIT 1 bug, there is no Buildozer .spec file so no APK can be produced from the repo, and the Cython performance layer described in the README does not exist in the code. The problem is real and critical; the solution is not yet functional.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: Repo-only Android app. No prebuilt APK is provided and the Buildozer .spec file required to compile one is missing, so the project cannot be built from source as submitted. No demo video exists. The live_url in the submission points to the Git clone URL, not a deployable artifact.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Offline BLE/Wi-Fi proximity scanning lets a single rescuer's phone detect trapped victims' devices with zero network infrastructure — the right idea for post-earthquake Venezuela.
gaps: demo-video
live-demo
screenshots
working-APK
branding
real-users

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Beacon Rescate detecta el teléfono de víctimas atrapadas usando BLE y Wi-Fi sin internet — pero necesita corregir bugs críticos antes de ser útil en campo.

name: BuscaChat
db_description:
# BuscaChat

# Objetivo del Proyecto

# ¿Como Funciona?

# Equipo

# Arquitectura

analysis.summary: BuscaChat is a production-deployed WhatsApp (and web) chatbot for family reunification after the Venezuela earthquake. It syncs missing-persons data from the sosvenezuela2026.com API into PostgreSQL+pgvector, exposes a REST API, and drives a multi-step conversational flow across WhatsApp Evolution API, WhatsApp Green API, Meta WhatsApp Cloud API, and a web chat widget. The codebase is genuinely functional with real integrations, 4 versioned Alembic migrations, CI/CD via GitHub Actions + SSH + Cloudflare tunnel, a systemd service file, unit and e2e tests using testcontainers, optional InsightFace facial recognition with pgvector HNSW index, and Redis-backed conversation state for horizontal scaling. This is one of the strongest hackathon submissions in the cohort.
analysis.project_type: WhatsApp / web chatbot for missing-persons search and family reunification

database: PostgreSQL 18 + pgvector (HNSW index for face embeddings)
ai_ml: InsightFace (buffalo_l ONNX model, face embedding + cosine similarity)

architecture.pattern: Layered FastAPI service with adapter pattern for multi-channel messaging and external data sources
