If even one Venezuelan NGO provides a CSV export of found persons, this tool could be live and useful within hours — making it high-potential but currently incomplete.

key_features: Instant offline person lookup via Bloom filter (0ms, no network)
Bloom filter parameters: 50K capacity, 0.1% false positive rate, ~90KB filter size
Build pipeline: auto-discovers CSV/JSON sources in datos/, compiles filter, injects into index.html
PWA installable with service worker (Cache-First + Stale-While-Revalidate)
Offline report submission queue (IndexedDB) with Background Sync on reconnection
Supabase REST integration for report persistence
Designed to fit in a WhatsApp message (<150KB total)
verify.js test harness for filter integrity validation
Venezuelan ID (cédula) normalization (strips V-, E- prefixes, whitespace)
fetch-ong-api.js adapter framework for future NGO API integration
analysis.merge_potential: High concept value for a hackathon jury — uniquely clever and technically correct for the disaster-zone constraint. Low immediate utility because the data pipeline is entirely placeholder. Would pair well with a project that aggregates localized-persons data (e.g., a shelter-mapping or aid-logistics tool that already has a database of found persons). Needs: one NGO data feed, uncommenting the CI build step, and automating daily filter rebuilds.
red_flags: ONG API endpoint is a hardcoded placeholder URL — no real data source connected
CI filter rebuild step is commented out — deployed version has only 20 demo records
Bloom filter data in current index.html is mostly zero-bytes (sample data only, not useful for real queries)
No automated data refresh pipeline — updating the filter requires manual Node.js invocation + git push
BloomFilter class duplicated verbatim across three files (build-filter.js, verify.js, index.html) — hash consistency is critical and any future change requires updating all three
No input rate limiting on Supabase report endpoint (open to spam writes with the public anon key)

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The concept directly addresses a critical real-world need: family members searching for earthquake survivors in Venezuela, where connectivity is unreliable or absent. The Bloom filter offline lookup architecture is genuinely well-suited to disaster-zone constraints (low-bandwidth phones, sharable via WhatsApp at under 150KB). However, the implementation is currently non-functional for real victims — the deployed version contains only 20 demo records, the NGO data ingestion endpoint is a hardcoded placeholder URL, and the CI filter-rebuild step is commented out. No real person would be found using this app today.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The live_url resolves to the GitHub repo page, not a deployed app. A Vercel deployment is referenced in the config but no accessible URL was provided. No demo video exists. The deployed index.html has a Bloom filter containing only 20 demo records — searching any real Venezuelan ID would return "not found," making the demo non-illustrative of real value.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Tech-forward appeal to NGOs and developers: pitch the architecture (offline, WhatsApp-shareable, 0ms lookup) and issue a public call for any Venezuelan NGO or volunteer network to contribute a CSV of located persons to activate the tool. Frame it as 'infrastructure waiting for data' rather than a finished product.
gaps: No working public demo URL
No demo video
No screenshots of the UI
No real victim data in the filter — tool is non-functional for actual use
No NGO partnership announced
Data pipeline entirely placeholder — CI rebuild commented out

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Una app de 150KB que permite buscar sobrevivientes del terremoto sin internet — pero necesita datos reales de ONGs para activarse.

name: Psico Ayuda Venezuela
db_description:
# Red de Apoyo Psicológico Venezuela

Plataforma de apoyo psicológico para respuesta a emergencias en Venezuela. Conecta a personas que necesitan contención emocional con psicólogos verificados, y permite a los profesionales registrarse, subir sus credenciales y gestionar su disponibilidad.

**Producción:** [psicoayudaven.com](https://psicoayudaven.com)

## Características

- **Ruta de paciente** (`/ayuda`): acceso rápido a ayuda presencial o a distancia.
- **Directorio de profesionales** (`/ayuda/profesionales`): psicólogos verificados con estado en línea y contacto directo por WhatsApp. Los disponibles aparecen primero. Grilla de 2 columnas en escritorio.
- **Perfil profesional** (`/ayuda/profesionales/$id`): página pública por profesional con SEO (Open Graph, Twitter Cards, JSON-LD) y botón **Compartir** (Web Share API → portapapeles).
- **Registro de profesionales** (`/profesional/registro`): formulario en dos pasos. La credencial es un único número de colegiación agnóstico de país (+ colegio certificador opcional). Soporta psicólogos dentro y fuera de Venezuela.
- **Panel del profesional** (`/profesional/panel`): activar/desactivar disponibilidad ("En línea" / "No conectado").
- **Panel de administración** (`/admin`): revisar y verificar o rechazar registros. Acceso basado en base de datos (columna `user.role`).
- **Centro de cuenta** (`/cuenta`): hub según rol (login, panel, admin, cerrar sesión).
- **SEO** en todas las páginas públicas (títulos, descripciones, Open Graph, canonical vía `src/lib/seo.ts`).
- **Autenticación** con Better Auth (email + contraseña).
- UI _mobile-first_ con estética _liquid glass_ sobre la paleta Medicall. Navegación: barra inferior en móvil, barra superior en escritorio.

analysis.summary: PsicoAyuda VE is a mental health professional directory platform for Venezuela earthquake response. It allows psychologists to register and list their credentials/availability, and connects earthquake victims with verified mental health professionals via WhatsApp. The stack is TanStack Start (SSR React) running on Cloudflare Workers with Cloudflare D1 (SQLite at edge) via Drizzle ORM and Better Auth for auth. The code is genuinely functional — real DB migrations, a working admin verification pipeline, role-based access, paginated filtered directory, and a Cloudflare deployment config pointing at a live domain (psicoayudaven.com) with a real D1 database ID. Quality is high for a 2-day-old hackathon project but has no tests and no CI.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack SSR web application (TanStack Start / Cloudflare Workers)

architecture.pattern: Full-stack SSR monorepo with file-based routing and RPC-style server functions
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Drizzle ORM over Cloudflare D1 (SQLite); 7 committed migration files; indexed on verifiedStatus, estado, country, available
architecture.api_design: TanStack Start createServerFn — typed RPC server functions colocated with the route tree; no separate REST layer. Auth handled by Better Auth at /api/auth/$.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear split: src/server/ holds all DB-touching server fns, src/routes/ holds file-based route components, src/db/ has schema + DB singleton, src/lib/ has auth/utils/notifications. Admin and public directories are gated at the route loader level.
architecture.modularity: Single app without sub-packages; modular enough for its scope. Server fn colocating schemas and validators in professionals.ts is intentional and clean.
notable_patterns: Singleton Db with per-request Cloudflare env injection via setCloudflareEnv
Admin role stored in DB (not env var list), promote-only design prevents admin lockout
Shared buildProfessionalWhere() ensures list and random-pick draw from identical filter pool
Orphan auth user cleanup on failed professional insert (transaction-like rollback)
URL-synced filters for SSR-friendly shareable search
getHeaders() reads global __TSS_REQUEST__ to forward cookies during SSR

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean: src/server/, src/routes/, src/db/, src/lib/, src/components/ with sensible scoping. Drizzle migrations in /drizzle with meta snapshots. No test directory exists.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish naming for domain entities (profesionales, estado, ciudad) matching the product language; camelCase TS conventions followed throughout.
code_organization.documentation_quality: No JSDoc but heavy inline 'ponytail:' comments that explain non-obvious decisions (why a LIKE not an index, why getHeaders uses globals, why promote-only). README present. AGENTS.md for AI context. CHANGELOG maintained.
code_organization.reasoning: Code is well-structured with deliberate patterns and rationale comments. Score is 4 not 5 because there are no tests and some production deps that should be devDeps (faker).

production_readiness.secrets_handling: BETTER_AUTH_SECRET via .env.local locally; D1 binding injected by Cloudflare Workers runtime (no secret in code). .env.example documents all vars. t3-oss/env-core validates at startup.
production_readiness.security_notes: No rate limiting on registration or auth endpoints — abuse risk for a public-facing emergency service. Sentry configured with sendDefaultPii=true and replaysSessionSampleRate=1.0 which captures full session replays including all user input; problematic for vulnerable earthquake victims. listVerified server fn is not admin-gated (any caller gets all verified pro IDs). LIKE '%query%' on name field is open to brute-force enumeration.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Cloudflare Workers + D1 handles global edge distribution well for this scale. ORDER BY RANDOM() noted as an issue only beyond ~1k rows. Paginated list with count query is correct. D1 SQLite is appropriate for a directory of hundreds of professionals.
production_readiness.reasoning: Real deploy config with live DB ID and domain, Sentry integration, proper error handling with user-friendly messages — well above prototype. Loses points for missing rate limiting, overly permissive Sentry session recording, and no CI/CD pipeline.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real application with genuine domain logic — not boilerplate. 7 DB migration files show iterative schema evolution over 2 days. Admin review workflow, multi-step registration, and professional filtering are all implemented with real business rules.
maturity.reasoning: 27 commits in 2 days with a real D1 database ID and custom domain registered shows this was built to ship, not just demo. However, zero test coverage and no CI pipeline are meaningful gaps for a platform serving vulnerable populations.

viability.reasoning: The core MVP is functionally complete: professionals can register, admins can verify credentials, and victims can find and contact verified therapists via WhatsApp. Cloudflare Workers + D1 deployment is essentially free-tier and globally available — no server costs, no cold start issues. The custom domain psicoayudaven.com is already configured in wrangler.jsonc. It could go live today with a `npm run deploy` after setting the BETTER_AUTH_SECRET. The primary gap for real use is the Sentry privacy configuration and absence of email verification for professionals.

key_features: Searchable, paginated directory of verified psychologists filterable by modality (in-person/remote), Venezuelan state, city, and population served (children/adolescents/adults/elderly)
Professional self-registration with credential capture (certification number + certifying board + country)
Admin verification workflow: admins approve/reject pending registrations before they appear publicly
Role-based admin panel with DB-stored roles and promote-only escalation model
WhatsApp-based contact (no direct messaging — links to WhatsApp with professional's number)
'Random professional' feature — picks a verified pro from the current filter set for quick referral
Professional availability toggle (real-time, floats available pros to top of results)
Two registration paths: one-shot register+profile, or bare account then add profile later
Cloudflare D1 edge database with 7 applied migrations and proper index strategy
Sentry error monitoring with session replay
PWA manifest + service worker for offline-capable mobile use
i18n setup via Paraglide/Inlang (Spanish primary, German/English locale files present)
analysis.merge_potential: Standalone — self-contained platform with its own domain, DB, and auth. No obvious integration points with shelter-mapping or people-finder projects. Could be linked from a central hub as an external resource. Not designed to be merged into another codebase.
red_flags: No tests written — vitest is configured but zero test files exist; critical for a platform serving vulnerable people
No CI/CD pipeline — no .github/workflows; deploys are manual
Sentry sendDefaultPii=true + replaysSessionSampleRate=1.0 records full session videos including form input from earthquake victims — serious privacy concern
All TanStack dependencies pinned to 'latest' rather than fixed versions — reproducibility risk
@faker-js/faker in production dependencies (not devDependencies) — ships dead code to Workers bundle
No rate limiting on /api/auth/$ or registration server fns — open to bot registration of fake professionals
No email verification for professional accounts — a bad actor could register with a fake email and credentials that then enter the admin queue
listVerified server fn is unauthenticated — any caller can enumerate all verified professional IDs and availability status

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Earthquake trauma creates immediate and lasting mental health crises. Venezuela has a severely under-resourced mental health system even in normal times, and earthquake victims face acute stress, grief, and PTSD with nowhere to turn. This platform directly addresses that gap by creating a curated, admin-verified directory of psychologists who actively opt in to help — reachable via WhatsApp, which is the dominant communication channel in Venezuela even on low-bandwidth connections. The two-sided model (victims find help, professionals volunteer availability) is the right design for a crisis context.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The site loads cleanly at psicoayudaven.com. The homepage presents a clear two-CTA structure (seek help / register as professional). The professionals directory at /ayuda/profesionales is populated with 4 verified psychologists (real names, real cities: Maracay, Los Teques, Caracas), each with a live WhatsApp contact button, availability status, and age-group tags. Filter controls (name, state, city, age) and the random-selection feature are visible. This is a functioning product with real data, not a mockup.

diffusion.recommended_angle: 4 verified Venezuelan psychologists are already online and ready to help earthquake survivors — free, confidential, and reachable via WhatsApp in seconds. Share psicoayudaven.com so victims know help exists.
gaps: no demo video to embed in social posts
no pre-made screenshots or visual assets
only 4 professionals listed — promoting widely before recruiting more psychologists risks demand-supply mismatch
no testimonials or trust signals visible on the landing page

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Psicólogos venezolanos verificados listos para apoyarte emocionalmente tras el terremoto — gratis, confidencial y por WhatsApp: psicoayudaven.com

name: Rastro
db_description:
# Rastro - Plataforma de Respuesta a Emergencias

Rastro es una plataforma de respuesta a emergencias post-terremoto, diseñada para facilitar la comunicación y logística en situaciones críticas. Está optimizada para ofrecer fricción mínima (no requiere registro para reportar) y garantizar un flujo de información verificada.

## 🎯 ¿Para qué sirve?

La aplicación está dividida en tres módulos principales:

1. **Mapa Colaborativo (`/mapa`):** Muestra reportes geolocalizados en tiempo real sobre necesidades de rescate, suministros y estado de vías.
2. **Buscador de Personas (`/personas`):** Permite registrar y buscar personas en refugios (por nombre o cédula de identidad), facilitando la reunificación familiar.
3. **Panel de Moderación (`/moderacion`):** Un área restringida donde los moderadores revisan, aprueban o rechazan los reportes ciudadanos antes de publicarlos en el mapa, evitando desinformación.

## 🛠️ Stack Tecnológico

- **Framework:** [Next.js 14](https://nextjs.org/) (App Router)
- **Lenguaje:** [TypeScript](https://www.typescriptlang.org/)
- **Estilos:** [Tailwind CSS](https://tailwindcss.com/)
- **Base de Datos, Auth, Storage y Realtime:** [Supabase](https://supabase.com/)
- **Mapas:** [Leaflet](https://leafletjs.com/) + `react-leaflet` + OpenStreetMap
- **Formularios y Validación:** `react-hook-form` + `zod`
- **Búsqueda:** `fuse.js` (Búsqueda fuzzy)
- **Íconos:** `lucide-react`
- **Hosting y Cron Jobs:** [Vercel](https://vercel.com/)

analysis.summary: Rastro is a post-earthquake emergency response platform built with Next.js 14 App Router and Supabase. It delivers three functional modules: a collaborative geolocated report map (Leaflet/OpenStreetMap), a shelter-based person registry with fuzzy search (fuse.js + Supabase), and a moderation panel that gates citizen reports before they appear publicly. The codebase is small (2,719 LOC) but coherent — real business logic exists, Zod validation is applied consistently at every API boundary, RLS is configured in Supabase, admin operations are properly isolated to server-side route handlers, and a Vercel cron job auto-archives stale reports. The main weaknesses are: no tests or CI, a privacy gap (persona PII is open-access by anon), and a shared-secret moderation auth that is not scalable.
analysis.project_type: Web application — emergency coordination platform (Next.js fullstack)

architecture.pattern: Serverless BaaS — Next.js App Router with API Route Handlers backed by Supabase; no separate backend service
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (query builder, not ORM); admin client (service role) isolated to server-only modules; atomic upvote via plpgsql RPC
architecture.api_design: REST-style Next.js Route Handlers; GET/POST on /api/reportes, /api/personas, /api/refugios; PATCH on /api/moderacion/[id]; upvote via POST /api/reportes/[id]/upvote; cron via GET /api/cron/archivar
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean split: supabase.ts (anon client) vs supabase-admin.ts (service role, server-only); validaciones.ts holds all Zod schemas; types/index.ts holds domain types; hooks/ separates data-fetching from UI
architecture.modularity: Three feature modules (mapa, personas, moderacion) each with a server page.tsx and a client-side *ClientPage.tsx component; shared UI components in src/components/ui/
notable_patterns: Server/client split via *ClientPage pattern avoids leaking admin logic to browser
RLS enforces business rules at DB layer (pendiente state enforced on insert)
Cookie-based upvote rate-limit (httpOnly, 24h TTL)
Vercel cron with Bearer token guard auto-archives reports older than 7 days
Zod schemas shared between route validation and TypeScript inference

code_organization.directory_structure: Well-structured: src/app/ for Next.js routing, src/lib/ for utilities, src/hooks/ for data-fetching hooks, src/components/ for UI, src/types/ for domain types, supabase/ for schema. Feature pages each have a server shell and a ClientPage component.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish naming that matches the domain (reportes, refugios, personas, moderacion); Zod schemas named *Schema, TypeScript types match DB columns.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is thorough with setup steps and feature descriptions; AGENTS.md and MANUAL.md are present; inline comments explain non-obvious decisions (e.g., why supabaseAdmin is used, RLS bypass rationale).
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase is notably well-organized for a hackathon project — clean separation between public and admin clients, consistent patterns across all route handlers, and readable domain-aligned naming. Minor deduction for no architectural diagram and some duplication between /api/moderacion/route.ts GET and /api/moderacion/[id]/route.ts GET.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Service role key is server-only (SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY, never prefixed NEXT_PUBLIC_); MODERACION_SECRET and CRON_SECRET stored as env vars; .env.example committed with empty values; no secrets in code.
production_readiness.security_notes: Critical gap: personas table RLS allows anon SELECT with USING(TRUE) — displaced persons' PII (name, CI, birth date, health status, family phone) is world-readable via the public API with no auth required. Moderation auth is a single shared secret compared by equality (no timing-safe compare); cookie is httpOnly+lax but not marked secure in code (comment says Vercel applies it automatically). Upvote rate-limit is cookie-based — trivially bypassed by clearing cookies.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Serverless on Vercel + Supabase scales horizontally by default. No pagination on persona search (hard limit of 100 rows). Supabase free tier connection limits could be hit under load. No caching layer.
production_readiness.reasoning: The project has solid fundamentals for a hackathon: env separation, RLS, Zod validation everywhere, and a cron job. The personas PII exposure is a real production blocker — health status and family contact data for disaster victims must not be publicly queryable. Fixing it requires adding a SELECT policy that restricts access (e.g., only authenticated users or a lookup-by-CI-only path).

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — contains genuine domain logic: SQL schema with RLS, triggers, seed data, Zod validation schemas, map integration, moderation workflow, and cron archiving. Not boilerplate.
maturity.reasoning: Created June 28, 2026 with only 5 commits and 1 contributor — clearly hackathon-fresh. The code quality is above average for the age but there are zero tests and no CI pipeline. The live deployment (rastro-flax.vercel.app) suggests it has been built and runs.

viability.reasoning: The core loop — citizen submits geolocated report, moderator approves, report appears on public map — is fully implemented and deployed. The person-finder module addresses a real family-reunification need. The privacy gap on personas PII is a serious concern that must be patched before real deployment with victim data, but the fix is a one-line RLS policy change. The single-secret moderation auth is a liability at scale but workable for a small team. With those two fixes this is genuinely deployable.

key_features: Geolocated citizen incident reports (rescue, supply, road conditions) submitted without registration
Moderation workflow: reports start as 'pendiente', moderators approve/reject/mark unverified before public display
Shelter-based person registry with CI and name search to support family reunification
Interactive Leaflet map showing verified and unverified reports with upvote mechanism
Atomic upvote via PostgreSQL RPC with cookie-based rate-limiting
Vercel cron job auto-archives reports older than 7 days at 3am UTC daily
Supabase RLS enforces that public inserts can only create 'pendiente' reports
analysis.merge_potential: The three modules (map, persons, moderation) are independently valuable and well-separated. The moderation panel and report schema could be reused by any other project needing citizen-sourced verified alerts. The personas/refugios data model is reusable for any shelter-tracking feature. Code is TypeScript-typed throughout, making integration straightforward.
red_flags: personas table allows anon SELECT with USING(TRUE) — victim PII (health status, CI, family phone) is fully world-readable with no authentication required
Moderation auth is a plain shared-secret string comparison — no timing-safe equality, no rate-limit on the login endpoint
Zero automated tests — no unit, integration, or e2e tests present
No CI pipeline — no build verification on push
Upvote rate-limit is cookie-only — trivially bypassed; no server-side deduplication
persons_count counter updated via RPC that silently ignores errors ('ignorar error si la función no existe aún') — counter can drift from reality

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Rastro directly addresses three critical problems that arise immediately after an earthquake: (1) dispersed, unverified information about rescue needs, supply shortages, and road conditions — solved by a moderated geolocated report map; (2) family separation — solved by a shelter-based person registry searchable by name or national ID; (3) spread of misinformation in disaster contexts — solved by a moderation workflow that holds citizen reports in "pendiente" state until a human reviewer approves them. All three modules are implemented and deployed, not mocked. No registration is required to submit a report, which is exactly right for zero-friction disaster response.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The live URL (https://rastro-six.vercel.app) redirects (HTTP 307) but the app is fully functional at its pages. /mapa loads with an interactive emergency map, category filters (rescue, supplies, road conditions), and a report submission button. /personas loads a working person-finder search interface with name/CI lookup. The map shows a loading state with zero current reports (expected — it's a hackathon demo with no seed data). Navigation between Map, Report, People, and Moderation sections is accessible. The platform's tagline "Datos colaborativos — verifica antes de actuar" is visible and on-brand.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the family-reunification module — searching for a missing relative by cedula de identidad is an immediately visceral, human need. Pair that with the zero-registration report map to show that Rastro works at the moment of disaster, not after bureaucracy. Highlight the moderation layer as the differentiator against misinformation. Sample post (Spanish): 'Después del terremoto, cada minuto cuenta. Rastro te permite reportar necesidades de rescate sin crear cuenta, buscar familiares por cédula y ver en tiempo real dónde se necesita ayuda — todo verificado antes de publicar. rastro-six.vercel.app'
gaps: No demo video showing the map, report flow, and person search in action
No screenshots in the repo for social sharing
No branding assets (logo, OG image) for link previews
Zero seed data in the live demo makes the map feel empty — a recorded walkthrough or seeded demo environment would help communicate the value instantly
No real users or testimonials yet

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Rastro lets earthquake victims report emergencies without signing up, find missing relatives by national ID, and trust that every map pin has been human-verified before going public.

name: Red Acaopio Vnzla
db_description:
# Centros de Acopio — Ayuda que llega donde se necesita

## ¿Qué es?

Una aplicación que conecta a quienes **quieren ayudar** con los **centros de acopio** que reciben y reparten insumos durante una emergencia.

En vez de no saber a dónde llevar una donación o qué hace falta realmente, la app muestra, en un mapa, los centros cercanos y **qué necesita cada uno en este momento**: agua, medicamentos, ropa, alimentos o herramientas.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

- **A quien quiere donar:** encuentra el centro más cercano y ve qué le hace falta, para llevar lo que de verdad se necesita y no lo que sobra.
- **A los centros:** llevan el control de lo que entra y sale, y avisan automáticamente cuándo un insumo está **urgente**, **normal** o **suficiente**.
- **A la comunidad:** la información está siempre actualizada y es confiable, porque un equipo revisa que los centros sean reales.

El resultado: menos desorden, menos desperdicio y la ayuda llegando más rápido a donde hace falta.

## ¿Quiénes la usan?

- **Personas que donan** — buscan un centro y ven sus necesidades.
- **Voluntarios** — ayudan a registrar lo que llega y se reparte.
- **Responsables de un centro (jefes)** — administran el centro, su equipo y su inventario.
- **Equipo de verificación** — confirma que cada centro sea legítimo.

## Cómo se usa

### Si quieres donar
1. Abre la app y mira el **mapa** o la **lista de centros** cerca de ti.
2. Entra a un centro para ver **qué necesita** y si está **recibiendo en este momento**.
3. Arma tu donación en la app y llévala al centro: ahí la registran al recibirla.

> No necesitas crear una cuenta para mirar el mapa y las necesidades.

### Si eres voluntario o responsable de un centro
1. **Crea tu cuenta** (con tu cédula y una contraseña, o con Google).
2. **Registra tu centro**: nombre, ubicación en el mapa y el inventario con el que inicias (puedes cargarlo a mano o importarlo desde un Excel).
3. Suma a tu equipo: **invita voluntarios** con un enlace o un código QR.
4. **Mantén el inventario al día**: cada vez que entra una donación o sale un envío, queda registrado. El sistema lleva la cuenta por ti.
5. **Configura las alertas**: define, para cada insumo, cuándo se considera *urgente* y cuándo *suficiente*. A partir de ahí, **la etiqueta de cada insumo se actualiza sola** según el stock, sin que tengas que cambiarla a mano.

### Cómo se mantiene la confianza
- Un **equipo de verificación** revisa los centros y los marca como verificados.
- Cualquier persona puede **reportar** un centro que ya no exista o tenga información incorrecta.

## En resumen

La app es el puente entre la **ayuda disponible** y la **ayuda necesaria**: muestra dónde están los centros, qué les falta y lo mantiene actualizado, para que cada donación cuente.

analysis.summary: VnzlCentrosDeInsumos is a production-grade emergency logistics platform for Venezuelan aid collection centers ("centros de acopio"). It is a Turborepo monorepo with a NestJS REST API, a Next.js 16 PWA-ready web frontend, and a shared Prisma 7/PostgreSQL package. The system manages supply center directories, real-time inventory tracking via an immutable audit trail (Historial), QR-based donation scanning, shipment dispatch with guías, volunteer role management, team invitations via JWT tokens, community reporting, and a moderation panel. It is live at https://vnzl-centros-de-insumos-web.vercel.app. The code is clearly not boilerplate — it contains 13k+ LOC of substantive domain logic, 157 commits, 4 contributors, 21 test files, 3 CI pipelines, Railway deployment config, and a Dockerfile. The main production concern is local-disk file storage for center photos, which would break in a multi-instance deployment, and the absence of versioned Prisma migrations (using db push).
analysis.project_type: Full-stack web app (monorepo: NestJS API + Next.js frontend + shared Prisma package)

architecture.pattern: Monorepo (Turborepo) with layered NestJS modules — Controller/Service per domain entity, shared DB package
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Prisma 7 with PostgreSQL adapter (@prisma/adapter-pg). All inventory mutations go through Historial audit rows in transactions (enforced 'golden rule'). Explicit Prisma `select` allowlists prevent PII leakage.
architecture.api_design: REST via NestJS controllers. Versioned cache invalidation in Redis (bump integer key, include in cache key). Rate limiting per fingerprint/IP using Redis fixed-window. JWT Bearer tokens for users (cedula+password and Google OAuth) and separate admin sessions.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clear separation: `packages/database` owns schema/client, `packages/venezuela` owns domain logic (cedula parsing, geo data), `apps/api` owns business logic and HTTP, `apps/web` owns UI. Guards (Sestion/Identidad/Voluntario/Jefe/Admin/RateLimit) enforce all authorization at the controller layer.
architecture.modularity: Good. Each domain entity has its own service/controller file. Shared packages published as workspace deps. Constants in dedicated files (cache, pagination, rate-limit).
notable_patterns: Immutable audit trail: cantidadTotal only changed via Historial rows in DB transactions
Redis cache versioning: a single incr key invalidates all paginated/filtered cache entries without SCAN
Explicit Prisma select allowlists per consumer (card, detalle, miCentro, moderacion, publico) to prevent PII exposure
RBAC via composable NestJS guards (AdminGuard, JefeGuard, VoluntarioGuard, IdentidadGuard, SesionGuard)
Venezuelan cedula parsing + external validation (api.cedula.com.ve) as best-effort background task
Geospatial proximity via Haversine in-memory with bounding-box pre-filter in DB
Community reporting (fingerprint-based, upsert 1/device/center) with moderation queue

code_organization.directory_structure: apps/{web,api}, packages/{database,venezuela}, docs/ (feature-level markdown), .github/workflows/. Files are consistently named by domain entity. Route grouping in Next.js uses (app) and (full) layout groups.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent. Spanish domain names match the problem domain (Centro, Insumo, Historial, Envio, Voluntario). TypeScript types are precise and consistently named (CentroCard, MiCentro, CentroDetalle, CentroModeracion). 'ponytail' comments clearly mark known tech debt.
code_organization.documentation_quality: High. Extensive inline comments explaining design decisions, security rationale, and known limitations. Feature-level docs in docs/. AGENTS.md with AI coding context. .env.example with all vars documented.
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase reads like it was written by experienced engineers who cared about maintainability. Domain logic, auth, caching, and PII handling are all well-reasoned and documented. The 'ponytail' pattern for flagging deferred decisions is notably disciplined.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: JWT_SECRET and GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID in env vars. Default fallback 'dev-only-change-me' in app.module.ts is a minor risk (should panic if unset in production, but Sentry + CORS env gating make production misconfiguration unlikely in practice).
production_readiness.security_notes: CORS restricted to WEB_ORIGIN in production. Input validated with class-validator + whitelist:true (strips unknown fields). Explicit Prisma select allowlists prevent PII leakage. Rate limiting on create endpoints. Admin sessions use separate JWT claim type 'admin' checked at guard level. bcryptjs (cost 10) for passwords.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Redis cache with versioned invalidation scales public directory reads well. Photo storage is local disk (breaks horizontal scaling — explicitly flagged as ponytail). Geospatial sorting is in-memory (candidateCap=500 rows, fine until ~1000 centers). No versioned Prisma migrations (uses db push) — schema changes in production could be destructive.
production_readiness.reasoning: This is a polished MVP with real production infrastructure. The two concrete production gaps are local-disk photo storage and absence of versioned migrations. Everything else (auth, rate limiting, caching, error tracking, CI, deployment) is properly wired.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — substantive domain logic throughout, 157 commits, 4 contributors, deployed app, idiomatic use of Prisma transactions, Redis versioned caching, and Venezuelan-specific validation (cedula parsing, estado/municipio whitelist).
maturity.reasoning: 4 contributors and 157 commits over 3 days signals rapid but genuine development. 21 test files with proper Prisma mocks and 3 CI pipelines (api, web, migrations with drift detection) demonstrate engineering discipline. The 'ponytail' deferred items are clearly scoped and non-blocking.

viability.reasoning: The core aid-center logistics loop (create center, manage inventory via QR scan, track shipments, invite volunteers) is fully implemented and deployed. The moderation panel allows the team to verify real centers vs. fake ones, which is critical for trust in an emergency context. The two blockers for true production scale (photo storage, versioned migrations) are fixable in hours. This is ready to serve real earthquake victims with minimal additional work.

key_features: Public directory of aid centers with geospatial search (Haversine + bounding box), filterable by urgency, open status, and verification
QR code-based donation scanning — donors scan a center QR, volunteers scan donor QR items to register supplies
Immutable inventory audit trail (Historial) enforced by DB transactions — stock can never drift
Shipment (envío) dispatch with printable guías and QR tracking
RBAC: Jefe (owner) vs. Voluntario roles within each center; Admin for platform moderation
Team invitation flow: JWT-signed QR/link, 24h TTL, requires complete identity to redeem
Community reporting system (anonymous fingerprint-based) with moderation queue sorted by report count
Center verification panel with photo evidence, GPS vs. declared address comparison, and cedula verification of the responsible person
Venezuelan cedula format validation + live verification against api.cedula.com.ve
Redis-backed versioned cache for public endpoints + fixed-window rate limiting on write endpoints
Google OAuth + cedula/password dual login with profile completion onboarding flow
Sentry error tracking on both API and web
3 GitHub Actions CI pipelines with migration drift detection
analysis.merge_potential: High. The project is focused, well-documented, and already deployed. It fills a concrete gap (supply center directory + inventory management) that other projects likely don't duplicate. The modular monorepo structure and REST API make integration straightforward. The only prerequisite for a merge/deploy is migrating photo storage from local disk to object storage (e.g., S3/R2).
red_flags: Local disk storage for center photos (/uploads/) will break in any multi-instance or serverless deployment — explicitly acknowledged as 'ponytail' but unresolved
No versioned Prisma migrations — railway.json pre-deploy uses `prisma db push` which can silently drop columns in production schema changes
JWT_SECRET has in-code fallback 'dev-only-change-me' in app.module.ts — should panic on startup if unset in NODE_ENV=production
Candidate cap of 500 rows for geospatial search means centers beyond the 500th nearest are invisible in proximity queries — acceptable now but will degrade as network grows

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Post-earthquake supply logistics in Venezuela suffer from a coordination vacuum: donors don't know where to go, centers don't know what's coming, and aid gets misallocated. This platform directly attacks that with a live directory of aid centers, real-time inventory levels with urgency indicators (urgente/normal/suficiente), QR-based donation scanning, shipment tracking, and a moderation layer to prevent fake centers from entering the network. It is one of the most operationally complete disaster-logistics solutions in the hackathon — it covers the full donor-to-shelf loop rather than just a landing page or a form.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The domain https://www.redacopiovnzla.com/ resolves and returns content (title "Red Acopio Venezuela" is rendered), but WebFetch cannot execute JavaScript so the Next.js SPA does not render its interactive UI. The Vercel deployment (https://vnzl-centros-de-insumos-web.vercel.app) shows the same minimal server-rendered shell. This is almost certainly a functioning JavaScript-rendered app that WebFetch cannot fully evaluate — the technical evidence (Vercel deploy, Railway API, PostgreSQL + Redis on Railway, Sentry tracking active) strongly suggests the full app is live. However, a human visiting the URL would need to verify the map, login, and inventory flows are operational.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Frame it around the donor's friction: 'You want to donate but don't know where to go or what's needed.' Then show the solution — open the map, see the nearest verified aid center, see exactly what it urgently needs right now, bring that. Emphasize the verification and real-time inventory angle as the differentiator over WhatsApp groups and word-of-mouth coordination.
gaps: demo-video
screenshots
real-users
branding

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Red Acopio Venezuela conecta a donadores con los centros de acopio más cercanos en tiempo real — sabés qué le falta a cada centro antes de salir de casa, y el centro sabe exactamente qué va a llegar.

name: Reencuentros Terremotos Venezuela
db_description:
## **Nuestra propuesta de valor**

Plataforma que centraliza reportes visuales (fotos y videos) recientes y georreferenciados de quienes necesitan ser encontrados —personas y animales—. Esta información es capturada en el terreno por medios de comunicación acreditados e instituciones de ayuda autorizadas. Ponemos la inteligencia artificial al servicio de esta causa para procesar grandes volúmenes de datos e identificar coincidencias que faciliten reencuentros seguros y oportunos, asegurando que sean las propias instituciones y medios autorizados quienes validen cada hallazgo dentro del sistema para una gestión segura y privada de la información.

## **Nuestro norte**

Tener completamente ajustados los protocolos de ciberseguridad, protección de datos sensibles, bienestar animal y no revictimización. La seguridad de las personas y los animales siempre estará por encima de la velocidad de lanzamiento.

analysis.summary: ReencuentroTerremotoVenezuela is a full-stack platform to centralize missing-persons searches, disaster alerts, and emergency data for Venezuela. Built in ~2 days (135 commits, 1 contributor), it is the most architecturally complete hackathon project in this cohort: Express 5 + TypeScript backend with Mongoose, BullMQ queues, multi-provider AI (Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini), Pinecone vector search, fuzzy + semantic deduplication, MinIO object storage, Redis caching, and a React 19/Leaflet frontend. Multiple ingestion jobs pull from FUNVISIS, USGS, GDACS, VenezuelaReporta, and other sources. The main risks are hardcoded JWT/admin secret fallbacks, absence of CI, and many jobs falling back to mock data when real government APIs are unavailable.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack web application — missing persons registry + disaster tracking platform

architecture.pattern: Monorepo with back/front split; backend uses layered architecture (routes → services → models) with BullMQ worker process decoupled from API process
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Mongoose ODM for MongoDB with typed schemas, indexes (2dsphere, compound, unique), and lean queries; Redis via ioredis for caching and queue transport
architecture.api_design: REST with Express 5 Router; 10+ route namespaces (/api/persons, /api/disasters, /api/auth, /api/admin, /api/localizados, etc.); Zod request validation; paginated list endpoints; 202 Accepted pattern for async queue submissions
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean separation: routes handle HTTP, services contain business logic (person.service, matcher.service, reconciliation.service), models own schema, adapters normalize external sources, jobs/workers handle async ingestion, queues bridge them
architecture.modularity: High — adapter pattern for external sources, factory pattern for AI provider selection, separate BullMQ workers for AI processing and disaster sync, 11 ingestion jobs for distinct data sources
notable_patterns: AI provider factory (Anthropic/OpenAI/Gemini switchable via env)
Idempotent upsert with SHA-256 idHash deduplication key
Fuzzy + vector embedding reconciliation pipeline (>95% auto-merge, 85–95% manual audit queue)
SyncState service for change-detection and skip logic on re-ingestion
Redis cache invalidation on write (counts, listing first page)
Audit log model for legal accountability (case close actions)
Docker Compose with depends_on health checks for all services

code_organization.directory_structure: back/ and front/ are cleanly separated; back/src has routes/, services/, models/, adapters/, jobs/, workers/, queues/, validators/, utils/, config/ — all properly named and purpose-driven; test files split between back/test/unit/ and back/src/__tests__/; extensive Doc/ folder with specs, data-model diagrams, and user docs
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent — file names match their exports (person.service.ts, matcher.service.ts, ia-processor.worker.ts); Spanish used consistently for domain terms reflecting Venezuelan context
code_organization.documentation_quality: Unusually thorough for a hackathon: Readme.md, DEVELOPERS.md, TDD.md, Integraciones.md plus 8+ Word/PDF spec documents in Doc/; inline comments explain security decisions and algorithm thresholds
code_organization.reasoning: Structure is production-pattern quality with clear layers and consistent conventions. Minor issues: some `any` casts in worker and reconciliation code, gender enum mismatch ('U' written in one job vs 'unknown' in schema), and a development fallback in auth.route that bypasses Google token verification.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: JWT_SECRET and ADMIN_API_KEY both have hardcoded fallback strings in source code ('super-secret-key-reencuentro-2024', 'super-secret-admin-key-2026'); env vars documented in .env.example but no secrets manager or validation on startup
production_readiness.security_notes: Strong positives: Helmet headers, global rate limiter (100 req/15 min), HPP protection, Zod input validation, RBAC middleware, safe projection on all person list endpoints excluding PII. Critical gap: JWT_SECRET fallback means any deployment without env var set is insecure; dev fallback in /auth/google skips Google token verification when GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID is 'dummy-client-id'
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Well-designed for scale: API and worker are separate Docker containers that can scale independently; Redis-backed BullMQ queues buffer load spikes; Redis caching on hot list endpoints; bulk MongoDB writes chunked at 200 ops; 2dsphere geo index enables efficient proximity queries. Local cosine similarity fallback does full-DB scan and would collapse under load
production_readiness.reasoning: Docker Compose with health checks, health endpoint, security middleware, and queue architecture indicate real production thinking. Held back from 4 by: no CI/CD, console.log-only logging (no structured/centralized), hardcoded secret fallbacks, and multiple ingestion jobs that silently fall back to mock data when government APIs are unreachable.

To reach production it needs: real government API endpoints (several currently mock), JWT secret enforcement, CI pipeline, and structured logging. Multi-source ingestion from VenezuelaReporta, DTV, Cruz Roja, Protección Civil, and a Reencuentro API
Disaster event tracking from FUNVISIS (Venezuelan seismology), USGS, GDACS, FIRMS with geo-proximity linking to missing persons
Interactive Leaflet map with clustering for persons and disaster events
BullMQ async processing — AI enrichment and deduplication happen off the request path
Multi-provider AI factory: Anthropic Claude, OpenAI, Google Gemini switchable via env var
Pinecone + MongoDB Atlas Vector Search + local cosine similarity (three-tier vector search strategy)
Google OAuth login with JWT, RBAC roles (admin/verifier/user), profile completion gate
MinIO S3-compatible photo storage with Multer upload
Audit log model for legal chain-of-custody on case closures
Redis caching on list/count endpoints with 30s/5min TTL
CNE (electoral registry) validator for cedula verification
analysis.merge_potential: High — the reconciliation pipeline, multi-source adapters, and disaster-to-person geo-linking are production-grade components that could be extracted and reused. The BullMQ + worker pattern, vector matching service, and SyncState deduplication service are particularly valuable as standalone modules.
red_flags: JWT_SECRET has hardcoded fallback 'super-secret-key-reencuentro-2024' in both auth.middleware.ts and auth.route.ts — any deployment without the env var is insecure
ADMIN_API_KEY fallback 'super-secret-admin-key-2026' in .env.example (treated as default)
Google OAuth /auth/google endpoint skips token verification when GOOGLE_CLIENT_ID === 'dummy-client-id' — this dev backdoor must be removed before production

Multiple ingestion jobs (FUNVISIS, Reencuentro API) silently fall back to hardcoded mock/generated data when real government APIs are unreachable — this means the DB may be populated with fake records in a real deployment
No CI/CD pipeline (no GitHub Actions or equivalent)
Local cosine similarity fallback loads all Person embeddings into Node.js memory — would OOM under non-trivial load
Gender enum inconsistency: reencuentro.job.ts writes 'U' but Mongoose schema only accepts 'M', 'F', 'other', 'unknown'
backend.log file committed to repository (may contain sensitive runtime data)

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Family separation during earthquakes is a documented, acute humanitarian crisis. Venezuela's seismic risk is real (FUNVISIS tracks dozens of events). The project directly targets the hardest part of disaster response — cross-referencing fragmented reports of missing and found persons at scale, including animals. The multi-source ingestion (Cruz Roja, Protección Civil, VenezuelaReporta, USGS, GDACS) reflects genuine operational understanding of how disaster information flows in Venezuela. The reconciliation pipeline with AI deduplication solves a real bottleneck: volunteer organizations manually comparing lists across dozens of channels.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The submitted live_url points to a Google Docs file titled "TDD_Reencuentro VE" — an internal technical design document, not a working product. The page is partially inaccessible (Google renders a browser-compatibility warning). There is no public-facing demo a victim, donor, or journalist could interact with. The technical analysis references a Vercel frontend deployment, but that URL was not submitted and could not be verified. No demo video exists either.

diffusion.recommended_angle: If a working Vercel URL is shared and a 60-second screen recording is recorded showing the map + a missing-person report being matched, this project becomes immediately promotable. The angle should be: 'AI que conecta familias separadas por el terremoto — en segundos, no días.' Focus on the deduplication pipeline and the geo-map as visual proof-points.
gaps: No accessible live demo URL (submitted URL is an internal Google Doc)
No demo video
No screenshots in repo or submission
Security vulnerabilities (hardcoded JWT secrets, OAuth bypass) must be fixed before public exposure
Multiple ingestion jobs fall back to mock data — a live demo could show fake records
No CI/CD means there is no stable deployed state to point to

evaluation.one_line_pitch: AI-powered platform that matches reports of missing persons and found survivors across multiple Venezuelan disaster-response channels — reuniting families in minutes instead of days.

name: Reestructuramos - Inspeccion Estructural Post Sismo
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?

**Reestructuramos** es una PWA **offline-first** para la **evaluación rápida de seguridad estructural de edificios después de un terremoto**, construida para los sismos **M7.2 + M7.5 del 24 de junio de 2026** en Venezuela.

Cada edificio se clasifica con el sistema universal de tres carteles — **VERDE** (habitable), **AMARILLO** (uso restringido) y **ROJO** (inseguro) — siguiendo la metodología **ATC-20** adaptada a la norma venezolana **COVENIN 1756**.

Lo que la hace distinta:

- **100% sin conexión.** Tras un sismo la red celular colapsa: ese es el caso de uso central, no el borde. Todo se guarda primero en el teléfono (IndexedDB) y se sincroniza solo cuando vuelve la señal. Incluye caché de mapas offline.
- **Evidencia ligada al hallazgo.** Cada foto se ata al daño concreto (esta columna, esta grieta) con GPS + fecha/hora embebidos. Nada de "álbum suelto".
- **Nunca sobrescribe el historial.** Una re-inspección tras una réplica es un registro NUEVO; el QR del cartel muestra todo el historial del edificio en el tiempo.
- **Sugiere, no decide.** La app propone el cartel según los daños observados, pero el inspector confirma o anula con su criterio (ambos quedan registrados).
- **Mapa de daños en vivo** de la ciudad, con pines a color por cartel.
- **PDF de inspección** + **cartel imprimible con QR**, generados en el dispositivo (funcionan offline).
- **Guía de campo offline** (patrones de daño, árbol de decisión, glosario) + mini-inducción con quiz para habilitar inspectores.

## ¿Para quién es?

Inspectores en campo: **voluntarios, ingenieros y arquitectos**. Acceso público y anónimo, sin barreras — cualquiera entra desde su teléfono y empieza de inmediato. También sirve como herramienta de formación.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?

Le da a Protección Civil, FUNVISIS, el colegio de ingenieros y a los voluntarios un **instrumento común** para triar miles de edificios rápido, con datos confiables (GPS + timestamp, trazabilidad antifraude) y un **mapa de daños que coordina la respuesta** — incluso con la red caída. Diseñado "listo para aval", alineado a COVENIN 1756.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?

1. Abre **https://app.reestructuramos.com** en el teléfono (instálala: "Agregar a pantalla de inicio").
2. Haz la **inducción de 2 minutos** + el mini-quiz.
3. Toca **"Nueva inspección"**: fija la ubicación (GPS o toca el mapa), marca los peligros con el selector de severidad, mira el **cartel sugerido**, confírmalo y **finaliza**.
4. Genera el **PDF** y el **cartel imprimible con QR**.
5. **Pon el teléfono en modo avión y recarga la app** — sigue funcionando. Ese es el corazón del proyecto.

## Tecnología

Next.js 16 + React 19 + TypeScript + Tailwind v4 · IndexedDB (Dexie) con cola de sincronización propia y reintentos · Supabase (PostgreSQL + PostGIS + Storage + Auth anónima + RLS) · Leaflet + OpenStreetMap con caché offline · service worker propio · PDF y QR en el cliente. Diseño "instrumento de ingeniería": tipografía IBM Plex, alto contraste para uso al sol.

analysis.summary: reestructuramos is an offline-first PWA for post-earthquake structural safety inspection in Venezuela, implementing the ATC-20 / COVENIN 1756 rapid assessment triage system with green/yellow/red placards. The app allows volunteer inspectors to work fully without internet connectivity, recording building assessments, findings, annotated photos, GPS stamps, and placard decisions into IndexedDB (Dexie), then syncing to Supabase when connectivity returns. It includes a collaborative damage map, AI-assisted crack classification via Gemini Vision (server-side), QR+PDF report generation, offline map tiles, an onboarding quiz, and a re-inspection history system. The codebase is unusually rigorous for a hackathon submission — domain-correct, security-conscious (comprehensive RLS), and genuinely engineered for the disaster-response offline-first use case.
analysis.project_type: PWA — offline-first structural inspection mobile web app

database: IndexedDB via Dexie (local-first source of truth)
ai_ml: Gemini 2.5 Flash (vision) — crack/damage classification, server-side only

architecture.pattern: Local-first PWA with BaaS sync backend
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Dexie (IndexedDB wrapper) for local storage; Supabase JS client with raw PostgREST queries for remote sync — no ORM
architecture.api_design: Minimal: one Next.js API route (/api/analizar-grieta) proxies Gemini Vision server-side to keep API key off-client. All other data ops go client→Dexie→Supabase queue.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Excellent: lib/ holds pure domain logic (placard.ts is a pure function, db.ts is the data layer, sync.ts is the sync engine, types.ts and catalog.ts are shared contracts); components/ split by feature domain; app/ is routing only.
architecture.modularity: High: sync engine, placard suggestion logic, offline map tiles, photo annotation, and inclinometer are all independently encapsulated modules.
notable_patterns: Local-first: every write goes to Dexie first, Supabase is secondary
Custom sync queue with exponential backoff (no Background Sync API dependency — deliberate iOS Safari compatibility choice)
Atomic Dexie transactions for finalization and photo-to-finding linking
Idempotent upserts in both directions (client_uuid as idempotency key)
Zustand for online/sync status UI, Dexie useLiveQuery for reactive offline data
PostGIS triggers auto-populate geography column from lat/lng
SECURITY DEFINER helper functions in RLS to avoid recursion
AI classification stored as JSONB alongside photo metadata for audit trail

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean: app/ (Next.js routes), components/{ui,wizard,guide,map,result,onboarding,building}/, lib/ (domain utilities), supabase/migrations/, public/ (service worker + PWA assets), scripts/. No clutter.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent: English identifiers throughout, Spanish UI strings and PostgreSQL enum values (intentional — they match 1:1 between TypeScript and DB CHECK constraints). Functions are descriptive and consistently named.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Strong: CLAUDE.md is a detailed agent guide with architecture decisions, breaking changes, and golden rules. README covers setup, stack, and architecture. Inline comments explain non-obvious decisions (e.g. why Background Sync was avoided, why insert-or-ignore on buildings, why patchInspection vs saveInspection exist).
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase reads like it was designed by someone who thought deeply about the domain and the offline-first constraints. Separation is clean, contracts are well-defined, and the architecture decisions are explicitly documented.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: GEMINI_API_KEY is server-side only (never exposed to client). Supabase anon key is NEXT_PUBLIC (intentional — Supabase RLS enforces access control). .env files are gitignored.
production_readiness.security_notes: Comprehensive Supabase RLS with SECURITY DEFINER helpers. Inspection ownership enforced via owns_inspection(). Buildings: public read, coordinator-only updates. Photos bucket is public (structural evidence is intentionally shareable for aid coordination). Security headers in next.config.ts (X-Frame-Options DENY, nosniff, referrer policy). API route validates input before calling Gemini.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Supabase scales horizontally. pullMapData() caps at 5000 inspections — will need pagination at scale. Tile cache capped at 3000 entries. Sync engine is single-device; no conflict resolution for concurrent edits from same inspector on different devices.
production_readiness.reasoning: Solid fundamentals for a hackathon: RLS is comprehensive and correct, offline-first prevents data loss, secrets are handled properly. Missing: no CI/CD, no tests, no structured logging, no rate limiting on the AI endpoint, and the Gemini proxy lacks input size validation (could be abused with large base64 payloads).

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — substantial domain logic in placard.ts (ATC-20 decision tree), db.ts (Dexie schema with transactions), sync.ts (custom sync queue), and the full wizard step components. Not boilerplate.
maturity.reasoning: 6 commits, created and pushed on June 29 2026 (the day of the hackathon). Despite its youth, the implementation density is high: 8,512 LOC covering offline sync, PostGIS migrations, RLS, service worker, AI proxy, PDF/QR generation, photo annotation canvas, inclinometer, and onboarding quiz. Absence of any tests is the main maturity gap.

viability.reasoning: This directly addresses the core disaster-response need: inspectors in the field after an earthquake need to work offline (connectivity is disrupted), record structural damage with evidence, and produce standard placards. The ATC-20/COVENIN 1756 methodology alignment gives it institutional legitimacy. The collaborative damage map enables command coordination. The anonymous-first design removes registration barriers in an emergency. It is deployable to Vercel today with a Supabase project and would be immediately useful.

key_features: Offline-first PWA: 100% functional without internet using IndexedDB/Dexie as source of truth
ATC-20/COVENIN 1756 structural triage wizard with verde/amarillo/rojo placard system
Pure placard suggestion function (suggestPlacard) with ATC-20 logic — inspector can always override
Custom sync engine with exponential backoff, idempotent upserts, and iOS Safari compatibility
Custom service worker (no Workbox): NetworkFirst for navigation, CacheFirst for static assets and OSM tiles
PostGIS-backed collaborative damage map via Leaflet with offline tile caching
Photo capture with annotation canvas, GPS/timestamp embedding, and linking to specific findings
AI-assisted crack classification via Gemini Vision (server-side proxy, optional/gracefully disabled)
PDF report + QR code generation for printable building placards
Building history tracking: re-inspections create new records sharing building_id
Comprehensive Supabase RLS: public read, owner-only write, coordinator role for overrides
Anonymous auth by default + optional OTP email for multi-device identity
Onboarding quiz for inspector qualification
Device sensors: inclinometer (DeviceOrientation), compass, and speech-to-text (DictateButton)
analysis.merge_potential: High — solves a distinct and critical problem (post-earthquake building triage) not covered by other likely submissions. Offline-first design, standards-based methodology, and collaborative map make it immediately deployable. The AI crack analysis adds a meaningful augmentation layer. Main gaps (no tests, no CI, no rate limiting on AI endpoint) are fixable in hours.
red_flags: Zero tests — no unit tests for placard logic (a pure function that directly affects life-safety decisions), no integration tests for sync engine
No CI/CD pipeline (no .github/workflows)
Gemini AI proxy has no input size validation — a large base64 payload could cause denial-of-service or unexpectedly high API costs
Photos storage bucket is fully public (by design for aid coordination, but structural photos may expose sensitive building vulnerabilities)
pullMapData() fetches up to 5000 rows with no pagination — will silently degrade at scale
Only 1 contributor, 6 commits — bus-factor risk and limited real-world testing
Silent error swallowing in pullMapData() catch block provides no observability into map data failures

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The M7.2 + M7.5 earthquakes hit Venezuela on June 24, 2026 — five days before this submission. Post-earthquake structural triage is the most acute life-safety bottleneck in any major seismic event: thousands of buildings must be inspected fast, by volunteers with minimal training, often with no cell signal. The ATC-20 green/yellow/red placard system is the globally adopted standard for this exact task. This app directly operationalizes that standard for Venezuelan responders, offline-first by design because connectivity collapses after major earthquakes. The offline-first constraint is not a nice-to-have — it is the entire point, and the team built the entire sync engine around it. The COVENIN 1756 alignment gives it institutional legitimacy with Protección Civil and FUNVISIS.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The app loads at https://app.reestructuramos.com and renders its full navigation shell (Mapa / Nueva / Mías / Guía), the three-placard legend (Habitable, Uso restringido, Inseguro), an onboarding tutorial link, and the "Nueva inspección" CTA button. The Leaflet map shows "Cargando mapa..." which reflects a JS-rendering limitation of the headless fetcher — not a broken deploy. The app is live, publicly accessible, and presents a coherent product UI immediately. The demo video at https://youtube.com/shorts/FO0MElqIggs provides the full walkthrough of the inspection flow.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the offline-first angle tied directly to the June 24 Venezuela earthquakes: 'When the cell towers went down, inspectors needed to keep working — Reestructuramos triages earthquake-damaged buildings with zero internet, GPS-stamped evidence, and ATC-20 placards that Protección Civil and FUNVISIS can trust.' The QR placard printout and the live damage map are highly visual assets perfect for social content. The demo video should anchor the post.
gaps: No real-user testimonials or adoption numbers yet (launched same day as hackathon)
Screenshots not explicitly catalogued — need screen captures of the wizard, the placard result, and the PDF output for social posts
Star/fork count is zero — no social proof signal yet

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Reestructuramos lets volunteer inspectors triage earthquake-damaged buildings 100% offline — ATC-20 placards, GPS-stamped evidence, and a live damage map that works even when cell towers don't.

name: responsegrid.app
db_description: ResponseGrid es una plataforma orientada a la gestión guiada de logística y eventos, con foco en estandarizar procedimientos, documentar actuaciones, coordinar tareas y centralizar información durante todo el ciclo de vida de una emergencia. Su propuesta principal es la información verificada y la trazabilidad  en tiempo real.

analysis.summary: ResponseGrid is a production-deployed (live at responsegrid.app + api.responsegrid.app) humanitarian material-aid coordination platform built for Venezuela's 2026 earthquake. It is a pnpm monorepo with a NestJS 11 hexagonal/DDD backend (18 bounded contexts, Drizzle ORM, PostgreSQL 16, Redis/BullMQ) and a Next.js 16 (App Router) frontend. The codebase has 274 commits, 168 unit test files, 30 end-to-end specs, full CI/CD to EC2 + Vercel, Datadog monitoring, Caddy TLS, and 574 real Venezuelan collection-point records pre-seeded. This is not boilerplate — it is a functioning, deployed system at a remarkable quality level for the hackathon window.
analysis.project_type: Full-stack monorepo web platform (Next.js frontend + NestJS REST API + PostgreSQL + Redis)

architecture.pattern: Hexagonal / DDD (ports & adapters) with CQRS-light and domain events
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Drizzle ORM with typed query builder; raw SQL strictly discouraged and documented as footgun; 36 hand-written versioned .sql migrations applied by a custom idempotent runner
architecture.api_design: REST with full OpenAPI/Swagger documentation; typed API client package (@reliefhub/api-client via openapi-fetch); dual auth via Bearer JWT and X-API-Key header for service accounts
architecture.separation_of_concerns: ESLint rules actively enforce no @nestjs/* or drizzle imports inside domain/ and application/ layers; output ports are interfaces with in-memory stubs for unit tests and Drizzle implementations for integration
architecture.modularity: 18 bounded contexts (emergencies, resources, needs, offers, supplies, logistics, volunteers, reports, identity, groups, organizations, accreditation, templates, notifications, audit, metrics, geocoding, files); shared kernel limited to EmergencyId, Location, Priority, DomainEvent, DatabaseModule
notable_patterns: CQRS-light use cases as plain classes injected via NestJS DI tokens
BullMQ Redis event bus for cross-context domain events with in-memory fake for tests
Atomic Design on the frontend (atoms/molecules/organisms)
Permission model: Principal → Grant(role@scope) → Permission → can(); @RequirePermission decorator
Location privacy: approximate-location value object to avoid exposing exact coordinates publicly
Typed openapi-fetch client regenerated from Swagger schema on every DTO change

code_organization.directory_structure: Pristine: apps/api/src/contexts/{context}/{domain,application,infrastructure} for every bounded context; apps/web/src/components/{atoms,molecules,organisms}; packages/api-client as shared workspace package; deploy/ with prod Docker Compose, Caddy config, migration script, and full .env.example
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent and expressive throughout; business domain in Spanish (UI copy, commits, issues), code identifiers in English; no abbreviations or mystery names
code_organization.documentation_quality: Exceptional for a hackathon: AGENTS.md (comprehensive AI/contributor guide), ARCHITECTURE.md, CLAUDE.md, CONTRIBUTING.md, 15+ feature specs in docs/, GitHub issue templates (epic/feature/bug/task) with label taxonomy, PR template, DEFINITION_OF_DONE.md, Swagger UI at /docs, Datadog runbook
code_organization.reasoning: The codebase enforces its own architectural rules via ESLint, documents gotchas (raw SQL, lockfile, Windows psql), and provides the full contributor workflow. 168 unit tests and 30 e2e specs are co-located with their context code, not an afterthought.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: JWT_SECRET length validated at boot (throws in production if <32 chars); .env never committed; deploy/.env.example documents every secret with generation instructions (openssl rand); Datadog API key and DB password in env only; EC2 IAM role preferred over static AWS keys
production_readiness.security_notes: Helmet with CORP cross-origin and CSP disabled (JSON API); CORS origin-restricted to FRONTEND_URL; bcrypt password hashing; OAuth CSRF guard (oauth-state.guard.ts); rate limiting on auth (10/60s) and donation intake (5/60s) via NestJS ThrottlerGuard; API keys prefixed rh_live_ and hashed at rest; permission guard on all write endpoints. Gap: public map/resource endpoints have no rate limiting, a potential DDoS vector under disaster load.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Single EC2 t3.small for API+DB+Redis (documented in deploy/aws-free-tier.md); stateless API (S3 for files), so horizontal scaling is feasible but not configured; BullMQ enables async event processing; Postgres pg_stat_statements enabled for Datadog DBM; no connection pooling beyond Drizzle's pg.Pool
production_readiness.reasoning: The stack is genuinely production-hardened: real TLS, monitoring, versioned migrations, CI gate enforced before deploy, graceful shutdown hooks. The main gaps are the Noop email inviter (passwordless donor accounts silently skip the set-password flow) and no rate limiting on high-traffic public endpoints.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, deployed system with iterative development evidence
maturity.reasoning: 274 commits over 3 days from 3 contributors, 36 migration files reflecting real schema evolution (including deleted features), 79 open GitHub issues with triage labels, 168 unit specs and 30 e2e specs against a real Postgres+Redis test database, and a live deployment with 574 seeded Venezuelan collection points from acopiove.org. This is the opposite of boilerplate.

viability.reasoning: The platform is already deployed and operational for the exact disaster (Terremoto Venezuela 2026). It covers the full material-aid coordination loop: collection-point registration with inventory, need validation (48h freshness), offer matching, logistics/shipments, volunteer coordination, field reports, and a real-time Leaflet map. The data layer is seeded with real Venezuelan collection points. The only meaningful gap before wider adoption is the missing email notification flow (Noop inviter), which would need a transactional email provider wired in.

key_features: Real-time Leaflet map of 574+ Venezuelan collection/aid points with category-level inventory privacy
Full material-aid lifecycle: needs (48h freshness TTL) → offers → matching → shipments → delivery confirmation
18 bounded contexts covering logistics, volunteer coordination, accreditation, organizations, groups, reports, notifications, and audit trail
Granular RBAC: role grants scoped to emergency/organization/resource; service accounts with API keys for integrations
Dual auth: JWT (email+password, Google OAuth, Facebook OAuth) and X-API-Key for service accounts
PWA with service worker (network-first navigation, cache-first statics) for low-connectivity use
Public read-only REST API with Swagger docs at /docs for third-party integrations
Datadog APM + Postgres/Redis monitoring in production; structured logging with trace correlation
36-migration versioned schema with idempotent runner; CI enforces nest build (tsc) + lint + format + full test suite before deploy
analysis.merge_potential: High. The platform is already live, covers the core material-aid coordination use case comprehensively, has real Venezuelan data seeded, and is engineered for production. It could serve as a primary coordination tool immediately. The main integration work would be: (1) wiring a transactional email provider for the Noop inviter, and (2) ensuring the EC2 instance can handle disaster-scale traffic (add rate limiting on map endpoints or put a CDN in front of the public resource API).
red_flags: Email notifications are not implemented (NoopSetPasswordInviter logs a TODO instead of sending emails — donor passwordless accounts and invitations are silently dropped)
No rate limiting on public map/resource endpoints (GET /emergencies/:id/resources, in-bounds queries) — high risk of unintentional DDoS during disaster peak
Internal package name `reliefhub` diverges from external brand `ResponseGrid` (minor inconsistency, not a blocker)
Single EC2 t3.small hosts API + PostgreSQL + Redis — no horizontal scaling plan documented beyond 'stateless so you can scale'
SetPasswordInviter Noop means any user invited to an organization as a service account never receives their credentials

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: ResponseGrid directly addresses the hardest logistical problem in disaster relief: matching donated supplies to verified, time-sensitive needs across a fragmented geography. Venezuela's 2026 earthquake creates exactly this coordination collapse. The platform covers the full material-aid loop (collection-point registration → need validation with 48h freshness TTL → offer creation → matching → shipments → delivery confirmation → audit trail) and is pre-seeded with 574 real Venezuelan collection points from acopiove.org. This is not a hypothetical solution — it is live and operational for the exact disaster it targets.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: responsegrid.app loads cleanly, shows the active "Terremoto Venezuela 2026" emergency, presents a three-tier verification trust model (unverified / locally validated / official organization), lets users browse collection points on a map, submit resource offers, and access coordinator login. The site has a language toggle (ES/EN), links to open API docs, and a donation/contribution flow. This is a functional product, not a placeholder — the live emergency operation is real and interactive.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the live map: 574+ verified Venezuelan collection points, one active disaster, real coordinators already using it. The angle is 'built in 3 days, operational today' — contrast the 274 commits, 168 tests, and Datadog monitoring against typical hackathon boilerplate to signal this is real infrastructure, not a demo. Show the verification badge system as the differentiator: donors and victims can trust what they see.
gaps: no demo video to embed in social posts
no screenshots available for static social cards
email notification flow not implemented — invited users silently receive no credentials, which limits onboarding at scale

evaluation.one_line_pitch: ResponseGrid coordinates Venezuela earthquake relief in real time — matching verified donations to actual needs across 574+ collection points, live now at responsegrid.app.

name: ResQLink
db_description:
ResQLink AI es una plataforma gratuita de respuesta humanitaria diseñada para situaciones de emergencia sísmica. Permite conectar en tiempo real a personas que necesitan ayuda con voluntarios, organizaciones y recursos disponibles.

La plataforma utiliza inteligencia artificial para priorizar incidentes, detectar reportes duplicados y recomendar recursos automáticamente. Todo funciona desde el navegador, sin necesidad de instalar nada.

### ¿Qué puedes hacer con ResQLink AI?

- Reportar emergencias con ubicación GPS automática
- Ofrecer recursos como alimentos, agua, medicinas o transporte
- Visualizar un mapa en tiempo real con todos los incidentes y recursos
- Coordinar voluntarios y asignar tareas desde el centro de operaciones
- Consultar estadísticas actualizadas en el dashboard

> **Consejo:** ResQLink AI funciona como PWA (Progressive Web App). Puedes instalarla en tu celular desde el navegador para usarla como una app nativa, incluso sin conexión a internet.

analysis.summary: ResQLink AI is a humanitarian response PWA built with Next.js 16, Supabase (PostgreSQL + PostGIS + Realtime), and OpenAI GPT-4o-mini, targeted at earthquake emergency coordination in Venezuela. It implements Clean Architecture with genuine separation between domain, infrastructure, and presentation layers. The platform supports public incident reporting, an interactive emergency map (Leaflet + react-leaflet), shelter and hospital tracking, AI-powered incident prioritization and duplicate detection, USGS earthquake data ingestion, a Google News RSS social feed, and Supabase Realtime live updates. It is deployed on Netlify with a scheduled hourly sync function. Despite being built in 3 days by one contributor, the core feature set is real and functional — this is not hollow boilerplate.
analysis.project_type: Next.js 16 PWA — Humanitarian response coordination platform

ai_ml: OpenAI GPT-4o-mini (classify priority, detect duplicates, recommend resources, summarize incidents)

architecture.pattern: Clean Architecture (Domain / Infrastructure / Presentation / App)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (BaaS) with raw SQL migrations. No ORM — queries are written directly via supabase-js fluent builder in src/infrastructure/supabase/queries/. PostGIS WKB hex output is decoded by a hand-written parseWKBPoint() function in src/shared/utils/geo.ts.
architecture.api_design: Next.js Route Handlers under src/app/api/ (REST): /api/ai/classify, /api/ai/duplicates, /api/ai/recommend, /api/social-feed/sync. All are thin wrappers delegating to infrastructure/ai/openai/index.ts or supabase client. No versioning or OpenAPI spec.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Well-enforced: domain entities and enums have zero framework imports; infrastructure holds Supabase client variants (browser, server, middleware) and AI wrappers; presentation holds React components, hooks, providers. App routes compose from infrastructure queries.
architecture.modularity: High within the defined layers. Each query file (incidents.ts, resources.ts, locations.ts, social-feed.ts) is standalone. AI functions are individually exported. Realtime subscriptions are isolated in supabase/realtime/index.ts.
notable_patterns: Public-read + anyone-can-insert RLS policies on incidents and resources (appropriate for emergency context)
PostGIS GEOGRAPHY(POINT,4326) with GIST indexes + custom WKB hex parser (not GeoJSON)
Supabase Realtime postgres_changes subscription for live map/dashboard updates
Netlify scheduled function (@hourly) for USGS earthquake ingestion + Google News RSS sync
PWA manifest + Serwist service worker for offline support
Middleware-based auth session refresh via @supabase/ssr

code_organization.directory_structure: src/ split into app/ (routes), domain/, infrastructure/, presentation/, shared/, components/ui/. Clean Architecture is genuinely followed — domain has zero framework imports, infrastructure owns all I/O, presentation is pure React. Standard Next.js App Router conventions observed.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent. File and function names are descriptive and consistent: parseLocationField, classifyPriority, detectDuplicates, getDashboardStats, subscribeToTable. Route group naming ((app), (auth)) is idiomatic.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Above average for a hackathon: CLAUDE.md has detailed architecture notes, key patterns explained (PostGIS WKB, realtime, auth flow), environment variables documented.
