AGENTS.md present. MANUAL.md and PDF assets exist. README present but not inspected in depth.
code_organization.reasoning: The project genuinely follows Clean Architecture — this is not just folder naming, the actual imports and coupling respect layer boundaries. Documentation quality is unusually thorough for a 3-day hackathon project, including AI-agent guidance files.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: OPENAI_API_KEY is server-side only (accessed via process.env in API routes and Netlify functions, never NEXT_PUBLIC_). Supabase keys follow Next.js conventions. env.ts uses non-null assertions (!) without runtime validation — if vars are missing the app will crash with unhelpful errors.
production_readiness.security_notes: AI API endpoints (/api/ai/classify, /api/ai/recommend, /api/ai/duplicates) have no authentication or rate limiting — they are publicly callable and will charge the OpenAI account. The /api/social-feed/sync endpoint has optional CRON_SECRET guard but falls through to open access if CRON_SECRET is not set. RLS policies correctly restrict sensitive tables but allow public INSERT on incidents and resources (appropriate for the use case).
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Supabase handles scaling at the DB layer. PostGIS GIST indexes are in place. No pagination on incident queries (SELECT * without LIMIT). Supabase Realtime subscription model will work under moderate concurrent users. No caching layer; every dashboard load fires 5 parallel Supabase queries.
production_readiness.reasoning: Core infrastructure is sound: Supabase RLS, Supabase SSR auth middleware, proper env separation, Netlify deploy config. The blocking production risk is unauthenticated AI endpoints that could generate runaway OpenAI costs; this must be fixed before public deployment.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real application with genuine domain logic. Not boilerplate — the WKB hex parser, PostGIS schema, USGS earthquake integration, AI classification prompts, RSS social feed parser, and Realtime subscriptions are all custom-built functional code. The .claude/launch.json and AGENTS.md reveal AI-assisted development but the output is substantive.
maturity.reasoning: 15 commits over 3 days from 1 contributor. No tests of any kind, no CI pipeline. Seed data and migrations are present and detailed (004_seed_verified_data.sql). PDF docs (QA report, hackathon deck, manual) were auto-generated via Python scripts in docs/ — these are documentation artifacts, not code quality signals. The absence of tests is the primary maturity gap.

viability.reasoning: The platform addresses a real need with a working feature set: public incident reporting with GPS, AI triage, live map of incidents/shelters/hospitals, USGS real seismic data ingestion, and volunteer coordination scaffolding. The key risks for real deployment are: (1) unguarded AI endpoints need rate limiting or auth before public launch; (2) no pagination means the incidents list will degrade as data grows; (3) the news-to-death-toll regex extraction is fragile and could produce incorrect figures that get written as official incidents. These are all fixable in hours to days, making the core viable with targeted hardening.

key_features: Public incident reporting with GPS coordinates (no auth required — appropriate for emergency context)
AI-powered incident priority classification via GPT-4o-mini (critical/high/medium/low)
AI duplicate detection for overlapping incident reports
AI resource recommendation based on incident type and scale
Interactive emergency map (Leaflet) with color-coded markers for incidents, hospitals, shelters
Supabase Realtime live updates — map and dashboard auto-refresh on new incidents
USGS earthquake data ingestion via scheduled Netlify function (hourly)
Google News RSS social feed for Venezuela earthquake coverage
Hospital and shelter tracking with bed capacity and occupancy
Volunteer coordination with skill tagging and availability status
PWA with service worker (Serwist) for offline capability
PostGIS GEOGRAPHY columns with WKB hex parser for precise geospatial queries
analysis.merge_potential: Medium-High. The platform has a well-structured domain model (Incident, Resource, Hospital, Shelter, Volunteer, Organization) that could serve as a canonical data layer for aggregation. The Supabase Realtime infrastructure and PostGIS schema are reusable. The main integration concern is that all three AI routes are public endpoints — merging into a shared platform would require adding auth middleware to those routes first.
red_flags: AI API routes (/api/ai/classify, /api/ai/recommend, /api/ai/duplicates) have zero authentication or rate limiting — publicly callable, direct OpenAI cost exposure
No tests of any kind (unit, integration, e2e)
No CI/CD pipeline
env.ts uses TypeScript non-null assertions without runtime validation — silent crashes if env vars are missing in production
Death-toll extraction in sync-feed.mts uses regex over news text and writes results as authoritative incident records — this is fragile and could produce misleading official data
No pagination on incident/resource queries — table scans will degrade at scale
reporter_id hardcoded as 00000000-0000-0000-0000-000000000000 for automated USGS incidents — breaks referential assumptions
Single contributor, 3-day-old repository, 15 commits — very limited battle-testing

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Earthquake victims in Venezuela need rapid, decentralized coordination between people needing help, volunteers, shelters, and resources. ResQLink directly targets this: GPS-based incident reporting without registration, AI triage to prioritize critical cases, a live map of incidents/shelters/hospitals, and volunteer coordination. The USGS earthquake data ingestion and Supabase Realtime live updates make the platform operationally relevant during an active seismic event. The no-auth-required incident reporting is a deliberate and correct design choice for the emergency context.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The site loads cleanly at resqlink-ai.netlify.app and presents a Spanish-language landing page with three clear entry points: report an emergency, offer help, and access the live map/dashboard. Navigation to the map, dashboard, and login is present. The product is a functioning PWA deployed on Netlify — not a placeholder. Critical caveat: the AI classification/recommendation API routes are publicly callable with no rate limiting or auth, which is a direct OpenAI cost exposure risk before any real public traffic hits it.

diffusion.recommended_angle: AI-powered real-time disaster coordination — anyone can report a GPS-tagged emergency from their phone, an AI instantly prioritizes it, and a live map shows victims, shelters, and volunteers updating in real time. Deployable today as a PWA with no app install required.
gaps: No real users or usage data yet
No screenshots of the map/dashboard in action for social posts
Single contributor limits perceived credibility for institutional adoption
Security issues (open AI endpoints) must be patched before promoting to high-traffic audiences

evaluation.one_line_pitch: ResQLink AI conecta a víctimas de terremotos con voluntarios y recursos en tiempo real — reporta una emergencia con GPS desde el navegador y la IA la prioriza en segundos.

name: SARA Sistema de Apoyo y Respuesta Accesible para Emergencias
db_description:
# ♿ SARA
## Sistema de Apoyo y Respuesta Accesible para Emergencias

> **Una plataforma inclusiva que conecta a personas con discapacidad, voluntarios y organizaciones para coordinar ayuda en tiempo real durante emergencias y desastres naturales.**

---

## 🌎 Descripción

**SARA** nace como una solución desarrollada durante un hackatón para responder a las necesidades de las personas con discapacidad afectadas por un desastre natural.

La plataforma permite solicitar ayuda específica de forma rápida y geolocalizada, conectando a las personas afectadas con voluntarios, organizaciones humanitarias, profesionales de la salud y donantes cercanos.

Nuestro objetivo es garantizar que **nadie quede sin asistencia debido a una barrera de accesibilidad.**

---

# 🚨 Problemática

Después de un desastre natural, las personas con discapacidad enfrentan dificultades adicionales para acceder a:

- ♿ Equipamiento de apoyo
- 💊 Medicamentos
- 🚗 Transporte accesible
- 🏠 Refugios adaptados
- 🧏 Información accesible
- 🧠 Apoyo psicosocial

Además, la falta de coordinación entre ciudadanos, voluntarios y organizaciones retrasa la entrega de ayuda.

---

# 💡 Nuestra solución

SARA centraliza las solicitudes de asistencia en una plataforma accesible que permite coordinar la ayuda en tiempo real.

Cada solicitud se geolocaliza para facilitar la respuesta de voluntarios y organizaciones cercanas.

---

# 📱 Casos de uso

### 🧑‍🦽 Movilidad reducida

José perdió su silla de ruedas durante el terremoto y solicita una silla temporal o unas muletas.

### 🦻 Discapacidad auditiva

María necesita reemplazar su audífono y la plataforma la conecta con una organización especializada.

### 🧏 Comunicación accesible

Francisca requiere un intérprete de lengua de señas o información en un formato accesible.

### 👩‍🦯 Acompañamiento

Ana solicita apoyo para trasladarse de forma segura hasta un refugio accesible.

### 🧠 Apoyo psicosocial

Carlos necesita recursos de regulación emocional y orientación durante la emergencia.

---

# ⚙️ ¿Cómo funciona?

1. La persona crea una solicitud de ayuda.
2. Selecciona el tipo de necesidad.
3. Comparte su ubicación.
4. La solicitud aparece en el mapa.
5. Voluntarios y organizaciones reciben una notificación.
6. Se asigna la ayuda.
7. Se realiza seguimiento hasta cerrar el caso.

---

# 🆘 Tipos de ayuda

- ♿ Equipamiento de apoyo
- 💊 Medicamentos
- 🚗 Transporte accesible
- 🤝 Acompañamiento
- 🧏 Intérpretes
- 📖 Información accesible
- 🧠 Apoyo para personas neurodivergentes
- ❤️ Apoyo psicosocial

---

# ✨ Funcionalidades

- 📍 Solicitudes geolocalizadas
- 🗺️ Mapa de refugios accesibles
- 🤝 Red de voluntarios
- 🏥 Directorio de organizaciones
- 💬 Chat y seguimiento de solicitudes
- 🎁 Donación y préstamo de equipamiento
- 🚨 Priorización de casos críticos
- 📊 Estadísticas para la coordinación humanitaria

---

# 🚀 MVP

## Fase 1

- Registro de usuarios
- Perfil de necesidades
- Solicitud de ayuda
- Geolocalización
- Mapa de solicitudes
- Panel de voluntarios

## Fase 2

- Chat
- Notificaciones
- Directorio de organizaciones
- Gestión de donaciones y préstamos

## Fase 3

- Priorización mediante IA
- Dashboard para organismos humanitarios
- Datos abiertos para coordinación en tiempo real

---

# 🎯 Objetivo

Reducir el tiempo entre una solicitud de ayuda y la llegada del recurso adecuado, garantizando una respuesta inclusiva, accesible y coordinada durante las primeras horas de una emergencia.

---

# ❤️ Nuestra misión

Que cualquier persona con discapacidad pueda solicitar ayuda en minutos y encontrar una red de apoyo cercana cuando más lo necesita.

## Porque la accesibilidad también salva vidas.

analysis.summary: SARA (Sistema de Apoyo y Respuesta Accesible) is a hackathon project targeting aid coordination for people with disabilities after the Venezuela earthquake. It has a legitimately well-engineered Node.js/Express + PostgreSQL backend with parameterized raw SQL, real geo-proximity search (Haversine in SQL CTE), proper state-machine lifecycle enforcement both in application code and DB CHECK constraints, and a solid test suite using Node's built-in test runner. The Next.js frontend, however, is entirely a UI mockup — every page renders hardcoded static data, no form submits to the backend API, and the map page shows a placeholder comment asking for Google Maps/Mapbox integration. The frontend-backend integration gap is the single biggest risk for production viability.
analysis.project_type: Emergency aid coordination web platform (full-stack: REST API + Next.js frontend)

architecture.pattern: Layered REST API with module-per-operation on the backend; static-rendered Next.js App Router pages on the frontend (no data fetching)
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Raw parameterized SQL via node-postgres Pool. No ORM. Query construction is encapsulated in query.js and inline in route handlers.
architecture.api_design: RESTful resource routes: GET/POST /api/help-requests, POST /api/help-requests/:id/accept, POST /api/help-requests/:id/resolve. Consistent {data} wrapper on success, {errors[]} on failure.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Backend cleanly separates routes, validation/normalization, query building, and DB access into distinct files. Each sub-operation (create, search, acceptance, query, shared constants) lives in its own module.
architecture.modularity: High on the backend — shared constants/helpers in shared.js, discrete modules per operation. Frontend pages are standalone but carry no real logic.
notable_patterns: State machine for help-request lifecycle (open → assigned → resolved) enforced simultaneously at JS validation, SQL WHERE clauses, and PostgreSQL CHECK constraints
Haversine formula implemented as a SQL CTE with ROUND/LEAST/GREATEST guards for numerical safety — no PostGIS required
DB mocking via direct function replacement on the exported pool.query reference in tests — no external mocking library
Input normalization (trim, default values) separated from validation in every module
UUID validated by regex before any DB touch to prevent unnecessary queries

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean root split: backend/src/{routes,helpRequests,validation,config,db,server} and frontend/src/{app,components}. SQL schema in backend/sql/schema.sql. ai-specs/ holds human-readable change specs. Well above average for a hackathon.
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent snake_case for SQL columns, camelCase for JS/TS. File names directly reflect their responsibility (create.js, acceptance.js, query.js). No ambiguous names.
code_organization.documentation_quality: Root README is thorough on problem/use-cases/roadmap but light on setup. Backend README exists. ai-specs/ contains structured change descriptions that double as internal documentation.
code_organization.reasoning: The backend is genuinely well-organized with a clear layered pattern and good naming. The frontend is polished UI code with no business logic, which makes it look organized but provides no real value beyond the visual layer.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: .env.example provided; dotenv loaded in config.js. No secrets committed. DATABASE_URL validated at startup (throws if missing).
production_readiness.security_notes: No authentication on any endpoint — anyone can create, accept, or resolve help requests. CORS is fully open (app.use(cors()) with no origin restriction). No rate limiting. Acceptable design choices for an anonymous emergency tool but creates spam/abuse surface.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: PostgreSQL with a connection pool is solid. Indexed on status and created_at. Geo-search uses a CTE with CPU-only Haversine — no geospatial index. LIMIT 100 hard cap on all list queries. Single-process Node server.
production_readiness.reasoning: The backend is deployable as-is with a DATABASE_URL, and the schema/validation are production-quality. However, the frontend is entirely disconnected from the backend (all hardcoded data, form submits navigate to /chat via formAction instead of calling the API), which makes the product non-functional end-to-end. No Docker, no CI, no rate limiting.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real logic in the backend. The frontend is a UI mockup/prototype — no API calls, no state management, hardcoded mock data on every page including the map and request list.
maturity.reasoning: 20 commits over ~2 days. Backend has a meaningful test suite (8 tests across validation and route-level behavior with DB mocking). No CI/CD, no Docker, and the frontend-to-backend integration is entirely absent, making the combined product non-functional despite the backend being solid.

viability.reasoning: The backend is a deployable, tested MVP with real geospatial search and a well-defined data model for the core use case. The concept (geo-indexed anonymous help requests for disabled earthquake victims, with volunteer assignment lifecycle) is concrete and achievable. The main blocker to viability is the frontend: it renders no real data and the SOS form's submit button uses formAction='/chat' which just navigates to a placeholder chat page. Wiring the existing backend to the existing UI screens is the primary remaining work.

key_features: Geo-proximity help request search using Haversine formula in SQL CTE (no PostGIS required)
Help request state machine: open → assigned → resolved, enforced at app and DB constraint level
Eight accessibility-focused need types: equipment, medication, transport, companionship, interpreter, accessible_information, neurodivergent_support, psychosocial_support
Anonymous help request submission (no account required — critical for emergency access)
Volunteer acceptance workflow with contact info exchange
Input validation and normalization at every API boundary (trim, type checks, enum validation, coordinate bounds)
PostgreSQL schema with UUID primary keys and comprehensive CHECK constraints
Accessibility-first UI design with ARIA labels, Material Symbols icons, and keyboard focus styles
analysis.merge_potential: The backend module structure (routes/helpRequests, validation, query building, shared constants) is clean enough to be adopted directly. The geo-search CTE pattern and DB-level state machine constraints are worth copying. The frontend UI components (CategoryCard, PointCard, SOSButton, BottomNav, Navbar) are accessible and well-styled but need real data wiring before they can contribute value.
red_flags: Frontend makes zero API calls — all pages render hardcoded mock data; the SOS form submits via formAction='/chat' (page navigation), not a fetch to the backend
Map page (/mapa) contains the literal comment 'Integra Google Maps o Mapbox para marcadores en tiempo real' — no map integration exists
CORS fully open with no origin restriction (app.use(cors()))
No authentication or rate limiting — any anonymous actor can accept or resolve any open help request
No Docker, no CI/CD, no deployment configuration of any kind
Registration form (/registro) has no onSubmit handler and no server action
Urgency values in the frontend form ('alta', 'media', 'baja') do not match the backend enum ('high', 'medium', 'low') — integration would produce validation errors

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: People with disabilities are among the most under-served and highest-risk groups during natural disasters. SARA directly addresses a concrete, documented gap: disabled earthquake survivors struggling to access adaptive equipment, accessible transport, interpreters, and psychosocial support because no coordination layer exists for their specific needs. The eight need-type taxonomy (wheelchair, hearing aids, interpreter, neurodivergent support, etc.) maps directly to real barriers that emerge after a seismic event. The anonymous, geolocated request model is appropriate for the context — no account creation friction in a crisis. The problem framing is sharp and victim-focused, not generic.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The live_url points to the GitHub repository itself. No deployed web app exists. No demo video was submitted. The frontend UI is fully rendered in Next.js but makes zero API calls — every page shows hardcoded mock data. The SOS form submits via formAction='/chat' (a page navigation), not a real API call. The map page contains a literal comment asking the developer to integrate Google Maps or Mapbox. No end-to-end flow can be demonstrated today.

diffusion.recommended_angle: If promoting now, lead with the mission and the human stories (José losing his wheelchair, María needing a hearing aid replacement) rather than the product itself. Frame as 'a team building the emergency coordination layer that disability organizations have always needed — and they need your support to finish it.' Do not claim a working product exists.
gaps: No deployed demo URL — nothing a victim, donor, or journalist can click on
No demo video showing even the UI mockup in motion
No screenshots in the repo
Frontend-backend integration is entirely absent — the product is non-functional end-to-end
Urgency value mismatch between frontend ('alta'/'media'/'baja') and backend enum ('high'/'medium'/'low') would cause validation errors on first real use
No authentication, rate limiting, or abuse protection before any public launch

evaluation.one_line_pitch: SARA conecta a personas con discapacidad con voluntarios y organizaciones cercanas mediante solicitudes geolocalizadas durante emergencias — porque la accesibilidad también salva vidas.

name: Sismología VE
db_description:
## ¿Qué construiste?
Desarrollamos **Sismología VE**, un sistema de monitoreo y alerta temprana de sismos en tiempo real diseñado específicamente para Venezuela y sus regiones colindantes. El proyecto consta de:
1. Una aplicación web altamente interactiva y responsiva desplegada en Wasmer Edge ([terremotos.wasmer.app](https://terremotos.wasmer.app/)).
2. Una aplicación nativa para Android con un servicio en segundo plano que se ejecuta de forma persistente para recibir alertas inmediatas.

## ¿Para quién es?
Está dirigido a la población general de Venezuela, servicios de emergencia locales y cualquier persona que necesite notificaciones confiables e inmediatas de eventos sísmicos, especialmente en una región donde los canales oficiales suelen presentar demoras o caídas de servicio.

## ¿Cómo ayuda?
- **Entrega en tiempo real**: Utiliza una conexión WebSocket persistente directa con el EMSC (Centro Sismológico Europeo-Mediterráneo) y redes regionales (como el SGC de Colombia) para recibir y notificar sismos en cuestión de segundos, eliminando los retrasos de consulta tradicionales.
- **Mecanismos de respaldo (Resiliencia)**: Si la conexión a internet falla o el WebSocket se desconecta, el cliente cambia automáticamente a un flujo de respaldo (consulta al servidor de IONOS, lectura de caché local en localStorage y, finalmente, una base de datos local precargada).
- **Actualización de alta frecuencia**: Cuenta con un scraper optimizado en el servidor que descarga y procesa datos de FUNVISIS cada 20 segundos, evadiendo la caché CDN para garantizar datos frescos constantemente.
- **Diseño centrado en el usuario**: Incorpora filtros de búsqueda por magnitud y región, alarmas de audio configurables y un simulador visual para pruebas de preparación ciudadana.

## ¿Qué debería probar la gente primero?
1. Entrar a la web en vivo en [terremotos.wasmer.app](https://terremotos.wasmer.app/).
2. Probar el **Simulador de Sismos** integrado para generar un sismo de prueba y observar cómo reaccionan la interfaz, los marcadores en el mapa y la alerta sonora.
3. Descargar e instalar la aplicación de Android (disponible a través del banner superior de la web) para probar el servicio de monitoreo en segundo plano.

analysis.summary: SismologiaVE is a multi-component seismic monitoring system for Venezuela consisting of: (1) an Android app (Kotlin/Compose) that embeds a WebView loading the web UI and runs a foreground service polling USGS + EMSC + FUNVISIS every 60 seconds with real push notifications; (2) a Vite-built web app with an offline-capable Leaflet map (bundled tiles + GeoJSON), multi-source data fetch with localStorage fallback; and (3) a Python scraper running on IONOS shared hosting via cron every minute, converting FUNVISIS proprietary data to GeoJSON and serving it at a stable non-CDN URL. The system is actively deployed with real APK releases (v1.1.8, versionCode=10) and a live backend. A critical security issue exists: SSH credentials and the keystore password are plaintext in DEPLOY.md and build.gradle.kts respectively.
analysis.project_type: Android app + Progressive Web App + Python scraper — seismic monitoring and real-time alert system for Venezuela

database: None — JSON files on IONOS shared hosting (sismos_venezuela.json)

architecture.pattern: Polling + Static JSON distribution — no server-side API, data flows: FUNVISIS → Python scraper (cron/1min) → IONOS JSON file → Android foreground service + web app fetch
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: No ORM or database. Data is stored as GeoJSON files on IONOS and SharedPreferences on device for seen-ID deduplication.
architecture.api_design: No custom API. The app consumes three external GeoJSON endpoints: USGS FDSN, EMSC SeismicPortal, and the custom IONOS-hosted FUNVISIS proxy JSON.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Android side uses MVVM skeleton (ViewModel/Repository) but DataRepository is a stub emitting hardcoded 'Android'. Real logic lives in EarthquakeMonitorService and EarthquakeWorker. Web UI is a single main.js monolith with clear function separation.
architecture.modularity: Low-to-medium. The scraper, web app, and Android app are loosely coupled via the shared JSON URL. Android has both a foreground service and a WorkManager worker doing duplicate polling logic.
notable_patterns: Three-level offline fallback: IONOS live → localStorage cache → APK-bundled JSON
Bundled Leaflet map tiles (z7) and Venezuela GeoJSON for fully offline map rendering
Deduplication across USGS/EMSC/FUNVISIS using time+geo proximity threshold
Android foreground service with START_STICKY for persistent background monitoring
WebView JavaScript bridge (AndroidApp interface) for native notification control from web layer
APK distributed as Debug build intentionally to avoid signing conflicts on sideload updates

code_organization.directory_structure: Root directories are test/ (source), web/ (built output), app/ (Android), public/ (static assets). The naming is confusing — 'test/' contains the actual production source code, not tests. The Android module still has com.example.* package name.
code_organization.naming_quality: Inconsistent: production web source is in test/, the DataRepository stub hasn't been replaced, the Android package is still com.example.sismologavenezuela. Kotlin services and workers are clearly named. Python scraper is well-named.
code_organization.documentation_quality: DEPLOY.md is an unusually thorough operations document covering architecture, credentials, build steps, error table, and AI agent instructions. No README at root. Code comments are in Spanish, clear and helpful.
code_organization.reasoning: The project has strong operational documentation and well-commented business logic, but the source directory naming (test/ for production code) and leftover scaffolding (DataRepository stub, boilerplate UI tests) reduce the score. The Android MVVM layer is partly wired up but DataRepository never actually fetches seismic data — all real logic is in the service layer.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: CRITICAL: SSH password ('s!crapper1234'), SSH user/host, PHP cron token, and Android keystore password ('sismosve_pass123') are all committed in plaintext to DEPLOY.md and build.gradle.kts. These are now public in the GitHub repo.
production_readiness.security_notes: WebView uses deprecated allowFileAccessFromFileURLs and allowUniversalAccessFromFileURLs flags. Debug APK is intentionally distributed to end users. Cron endpoint token is exposed in public URL and DEPLOY.md. No HTTPS enforcement on the scraper's FUNVISIS source (uses http://).
production_readiness.scalability_notes: IONOS shared hosting with WordPress is the single point of failure for all data distribution. No CDN, no load balancing. The scraper is a simple Python script run by cron — no retry queue, no dead-letter handling. Would not survive a major earthquake event traffic spike.
production_readiness.reasoning: The monitoring logic itself (foreground service, deduplication, multi-source fetch, offline fallback) is genuinely well-implemented and deployed. However, plaintext credentials in the repo, debug-signed production APK, no CI, no env-var management, and shared hosting infrastructure prevent a higher score.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real, deployed application with 403 commits and versioned APK releases (v1.1.8). The scaffolding Android MVVM layer (DataRepository stub, boilerplate MainScreenTest with FAKE_DATA) is leftover template code that was never cleaned up, but the actual earthquake monitoring service is substantive and clearly in production use.
maturity.reasoning: Active development with 403 commits over ~2 days, real APK releases distributed to users, a live IONOS backend with cron scraping, and a thoughtful DEPLOY.md. No automated tests or CI pipeline exist; the test files present are Android project scaffolding, not functional tests.

viability.reasoning: The core value proposition — real-time Venezuelan earthquake alerts combining USGS, EMSC, and FUNVISIS on Android and web, with offline capability — is fully functional and actually deployed. The IONOS scraper + FUNVISIS normalization pipeline solves a real gap (FUNVISIS has no standard API). The main blockers for higher viability are the shared hosting single point of failure, exposed credentials requiring immediate rotation, and the absence of any monitoring/alerting on the scraper itself.

key_features: Real-time earthquake monitoring combining USGS FDSN, EMSC SeismicPortal, and FUNVISIS data sources
Android foreground service with 60-second polling and magnitude-based push notifications (alarm priority, vibration pattern)
Offline-capable web app with bundled Leaflet map tiles (zoom levels 5-8) and Venezuela state GeoJSON
Three-level data fallback: IONOS live JSON → localStorage cache → APK-bundled JSON
Cross-source deduplication using time window (10 min) and geo proximity (0.5 degree) thresholds
Python scraper that normalizes FUNVISIS proprietary format to GeoJSON with timezone correction logic
WebView–native bridge (Android JavascriptInterface) allowing web UI to control native notification service
Earthquake simulation mode for UX testing
iOS PWA support with step-by-step notification onboarding modal
In-app version update detection via version.json polling
analysis.merge_potential: Medium-high for the seismic data and alert domain. The multi-source aggregation (USGS + EMSC + FUNVISIS) with deduplication logic and the offline map tile strategy are genuinely novel contributions worth reusing. The Android foreground service notification implementation is production-quality. However, the exposed credentials must be rotated before any merge, and the IONOS dependency would need to be replaced with more robust infrastructure for a hackathon deployment serving earthquake victims.
red_flags: CRITICAL SECURITY: SSH credentials (host, user, password) committed in plaintext to DEPLOY.md — now public on GitHub
CRITICAL SECURITY: Android keystore password committed in plaintext in app/app/build.gradle.kts
PHP cron endpoint token exposed in public DEPLOY.md and URL
DataRepository.kt is a stub emitting hardcoded List<String>("Android") — the MVVM layer is unwired scaffolding
MainScreenTest uses FAKE_DATA and tests a function signature that does not match the actual MainScreen composable — tests would fail
Production APK intentionally signed with debug key — reduces trust and creates update signature conflicts
WebView uses deprecated allowFileAccessFromFileURLs and allowUniversalAccessFromFileURLs (security flags disabled)
IONOS shared hosting is a single point of failure with no redundancy or monitoring
No CI pipeline; deployment is fully manual via SSH
Source code lives in a directory named 'test/' creating significant confusion

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Venezuela sits on active fault zones (including the El Pilar fault) and experiences significant seismic activity, yet FUNVISIS (the national seismic institute) has no public API and its data is hard to access programmatically. Official alert channels are slow and unreliable. This project directly bridges that gap by scraping FUNVISIS data every 20-60 seconds, normalizing it to GeoJSON, and combining it with USGS and EMSC feeds to deliver real-time alerts via Android push notifications and a PWA — something the government has not provided. The offline fallback capability is especially relevant where internet connectivity may degrade during or after an earthquake.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The web app at terremotos.wasmer.app loads and renders a complete UI: time-range filters (24h/7d/30d), magnitude slider (0-6+), intensity classification buttons, Android APK download banner, iOS PWA install guide, and simulation mode. However, the data panel shows "Sismos Detectados (0)" with a "Cargando eventos sísmicos..." spinner — earthquake data is not rendering during the test. This may reflect a genuine quiet period or a connection issue between the frontend and the IONOS-hosted scraper JSON. The UI is polished and functional in structure; the data pipeline is the uncertain element.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Frame it as the earthquake early-warning system Venezuela's government never built: real-time alerts combining three seismic data sources, an offline-capable map, and an Android app that works even when connectivity is degraded — built by a solo Venezuelan developer in 48 hours.
gaps: Critical security issue (SSH credentials and keystore password committed publicly to GitHub) must be rotated before any spotlight — linking to the repo exposes live server access
Live demo was not showing earthquake data during review — needs verification that the IONOS scraper is running and the data feed is live
No demo video to show the notification system and simulation mode in action
No screenshots or visual assets for social posts
Debug-signed APK distributed to end users reduces trust for a safety-critical app

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Sismología VE delivers real-time Venezuelan earthquake alerts combining three seismic data sources — including FUNVISIS, which has no public API — with an offline-capable Android app and PWA that works even when connectivity fails.

name: TerraVE
db_description: TerraVE es una plataforma web que combina memoria ciudadana con visión computacional para responder dos preguntas críticas post-desastre: ¿Qué existía aquí? → Levantamiento ciudadano de infraestructura perdida ¿Qué quedó en pie? → Clasificación automática de daño estructural por foto El resultado: un mapa vivo.

analysis.summary: TerraVE is a Next.js 14 (App Router) + Supabase web platform for post-earthquake territorial intelligence. It combines citizen crowdsourcing of lost infrastructure (schools, clinics, pharmacies, markets) with AI-powered structural damage classification via a HuggingFace ViT model. Users can report a location before/after a disaster, upload photos that get classified for damage severity (no-damage → destroyed), and view results on a Leaflet map with a traffic-light color system. The app is deployed at terra-ve.vercel.app. There is no authentication; all data is publicly writable. The AI classification uses a generic ViT model (google/vit-base-patch16-224) rather than a fine-tuned earthquake damage model, making classification outputs unreliable for real disaster response. Two additional API routes (satelital, poblacion) query database tables not defined in the included schema, suggesting features that were planned but not fully wired up. No tests or CI pipeline exist.
analysis.project_type: Web app (Next.js full-stack)

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router monolith with BaaS backend
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Supabase JS client (@supabase/supabase-js v2) — fluent query builder, no ORM; direct REST calls also used in some routes (satelital, poblacion, dashboard)
architecture.api_design: REST via Next.js API Routes under /app/api/*; GeoJSON output for map consumption; CORS headers set on public endpoints
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean split: lib/ for domain logic (classify, geo, supabase client), components/ for UI, app/api/ for server routes, types/index.ts for shared types
architecture.modularity: Good modularity for a hackathon — classify logic isolated in lib/classify.ts, geo utilities in lib/geo.ts, Supabase client factory in lib/supabase.ts
notable_patterns: Pagination loop in /api/lugares to bypass Supabase 1000-row limit
Graceful AI degradation: photo is always stored even if HuggingFace is unavailable
PostGIS spatial index on (lng, lat) for geo queries
Venezuela bounding-box validation utility in lib/geo.ts
Traffic-light damage classification system (verde/amarillo/naranja/rojo/gris)
Server client reuses anon key instead of service role key — no privilege elevation on server side
PATCH /api/report/[id] accepts arbitrary body.update() with no field allowlist — mass-assignment vulnerability

code_organization.directory_structure: Clean Next.js App Router layout: app/ (pages + API routes), components/, lib/, types/ — all files in the right places with no clutter
code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent Spanish naming that matches the domain (lugares, reportes_dano, color_semaforo, clase_dano); TypeScript types and Supabase table names align well
code_organization.documentation_quality: README covers setup in 5 steps with SQL schema and .env.example reference; TerraVE_API.md documents the public API; inline comments are present and useful
code_organization.reasoning: Code is well-structured for a hackathon with 93 commits from a single contributor. Typing is thorough, separation of concerns is real rather than cosmetic, and the paginated Supabase query pattern shows deliberate engineering.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: HF_TOKEN and GOOGLE_MAPS_KEY are server-only env vars (correct). Supabase anon key is NEXT_PUBLIC (exposed to browser — expected for anon key, but the server client also uses it instead of a service role key, meaning no RLS bypass is possible server-side)
production_readiness.security_notes: PATCH /api/report/[id] passes the raw request body directly to supabase.update() with no field allowlist — mass-assignment vulnerability. No auth on any write endpoint. RLS policies are open-insert for all tables. No rate limiting on image upload or AI classification endpoints.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Vercel + Supabase scales horizontally for read traffic. The classify route sends base64 images in JSON (no streaming) which will hit Vercel's 4.5 MB body limit under load. No caching on classification results.
production_readiness.reasoning: Core error handling is present (try/catch on all routes, graceful AI degradation) and env config is clean, but there is zero authentication, a mass-assignment bug in the PATCH route, no rate limiting on AI/upload endpoints, and two additional API routes (satelital, poblacion) query Supabase tables not present in the committed schema.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — 93 commits, functional multi-page app with real business logic, live Vercel deployment, actual Supabase schema with PostGIS indexes
maturity.reasoning: The app is genuinely functional with real domain logic and a live deployment, not just a scaffolded skeleton. However, zero tests and no CI pipeline are significant gaps for a system intended for real disaster response where data integrity matters.

viability.reasoning: The citizen crowdsourcing and structural damage photo-upload flow is fully functional and genuinely useful for disaster response (collect pre/post infrastructure photos, classify damage, map results). The critical weakness is the AI classification: google/vit-base-patch16-224 is a general-purpose ImageNet classifier, not a structural damage model, so its outputs for earthquake damage will be unreliable. Switching to a fine-tuned model (e.g., from xView2 or RescueNet datasets) would make the AI feature trustworthy. The rest of the platform — citizen reporting, mapping, GeoJSON export — could work as-is.

key_features: Citizen crowdsourcing: report pre-disaster infrastructure (schools, clinics, pharmacies, markets) with before-photos and GPS coordinates
AI structural damage classification via HuggingFace ViT model — traffic-light severity (no damage → destroyed)
Graceful AI degradation: photos are saved even when HuggingFace is unavailable
Interactive Leaflet map with color-coded damage markers and filtering by type/status
PostGIS-indexed PostgreSQL via Supabase with paginated queries for large datasets
Satellite damage data endpoint (dano_satelital table) and exposed population data (poblacion_expuesta) for MMI-level impact assessment
GeoJSON API endpoint for map consumption
Venezuela bounding-box validation for submitted GPS coordinates
Supabase Storage for before/after photo persistence
analysis.merge_potential: The concept is unique and complementary — no other submitted project combines pre-disaster memory mapping with post-disaster AI damage classification on a single geo platform. Worth integrating the citizen reporting + damage mapping feature. The AI classification pipeline needs a fine-tuned model swap before it is trustworthy in production.
red_flags: AI model (google/vit-base-patch16-224) is a generic ImageNet classifier, not fine-tuned for structural earthquake damage — classification results will be meaningless in real use
PATCH /api/report/[id] passes raw request body to supabase.update() with no field allowlist — mass-assignment vulnerability allows any authenticated-less caller to overwrite any column
No authentication on any write endpoint; Supabase RLS policies explicitly allow unrestricted public insert — database can be spammed with junk reports
No rate limiting on /api/classify (HuggingFace calls) or image upload — HF token can be exhausted by a single malicious user
satelital and poblacion API routes query tables (dano_satelital, poblacion_expuesta) that are NOT defined in the committed supabase-schema.sql — those features will 404 on a fresh deploy
Server Supabase client uses anon key (not service role key) so server-side code has the same permissions as an anonymous browser user — RLS bypass is impossible
No tests and no CI pipeline for a system meant to guide disaster relief decisions

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: After an earthquake, two critical information gaps emerge: (1) what infrastructure existed before the disaster (schools, clinics, pharmacies, markets) so reconstruction can be planned, and (2) which structures are still standing versus destroyed so rescue and relief can be directed. TerraVE directly targets both gaps through citizen crowdsourcing of pre-disaster locations and AI-powered damage classification from uploaded photos. This is a real, documented problem in Venezuelan earthquake response where informal settlements have no official infrastructure records.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The site loads cleanly at terra-ve.vercel.app with a professional landing page in Spanish, navigation links to Map, Report, and Damage Classification sections, and a clear two-question value proposition. However, the three key metrics (registered locations, evaluated structures, critical zones) display as dashes, indicating an empty or disconnected database. The app is navigable and presentable, but there is no real data in the live demo to demonstrate the crowdsourcing or mapping features working end-to-end. The AI damage classification uses google/vit-base-patch16-224, a generic ImageNet model not fine-tuned for structural earthquake damage, meaning classification outputs shown to users will be unreliable.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Lead with the citizen memory-mapping concept — 'Venezuela no puede perder la memoria de su infraestructura' — and the interactive Leaflet map. Avoid highlighting the AI damage classification in promotional content until the model is swapped for a fine-tuned earthquake damage classifier. Show the reporting form and map UI rather than the metrics (which are empty). Best positioned as 'la plataforma que preserva la memoria territorial de Venezuela antes y después de cada sismo.'
gaps: No real data in live demo — metrics show dashes, making the map feature invisible to a first visitor
No demo video showing the full flow (report a location, upload a photo, see the map update)
AI classification is a generic ImageNet model — promoting this feature would mislead users about reliability
No screenshots of the map with populated data points
Zero community traction (0 stars, 0 forks, 1 contributor)

evaluation.one_line_pitch: TerraVE es un mapa vivo post-terremoto que combina memoria ciudadana e IA para documentar qué infraestructura existía y cuánto daño estructural quedó — para que Venezuela nunca pierda el registro de lo que fue.

name: Venemed
db_description:
VeneMed — Coordinación de donaciones para centros de salud, con orden y a tiempo.

Hospitales, refugios y centros de acopio reciben donaciones de forma descoordinada. Muchos insumos se desperdician porque nadie tiene visibilidad real de qué se necesita. El resultado es doble: recursos comunitarios desperdiciados y centros colapsados que ya no pueden procesar más ayuda.

Cómo funciona: Los centros publican solicitudes con ventanas cortas de tiempo (12, 24 o 48 horas) indicando exactamente lo que necesitan. Los donantes navegan la lista sin login: ven en tiempo real lo que cada centro está pidiendo y por cuánto tiempo. Esa información es compartible en redes, para que más gente sepa dónde llevar ayuda antes de que la ventana cierre. Si un centro entra en saturación, puede publicar un aviso de exceso para frenar donaciones de un ítem específico.

Para quién. El centro de salud (acceso por WhatsApp, panel propio para crear y gestionar solicitudes) y el donante (acceso público, sin registro). Pensado mobile-first y para funcionar con conexión inestable.

Objetivo. Que la ayuda llegue donde y cuando hace falta — y que ningún recurso comunitario se pierda en el camino.

analysis.summary: VeneMed is a time-windowed medical-aid platform built for Venezuelan health centers and donors. Health centers publish supply requests (solicitudes) with 12/24/48-hour windows; donors browse anonymously and share links that auto-stop circulating when the window closes, preventing wasted donations. Built in direct response to an earthquake. Three distinct surfaces: public donor feed (ISR-cached for surge), center back-office (WhatsApp/SMS OTP auth), and admin moderation queue. Deployed at venemed.vercel.app. The codebase is dense, security-conscious, and substantially complete — not scaffolding.
analysis.project_type: Web platform (Next.js full-stack)

architecture.pattern: Next.js App Router with route-group separation (public/center/admin), RSC-first, Server Actions for mutations
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: Drizzle ORM with postgres-js driver (bypasses Supabase RLS intentionally; authorization enforced in server code)
architecture.api_design: Server Actions for all mutations (no REST layer); one API route /api/cron/expire-requests for cron; all reads via Server Components + unstable_cache with tag-based invalidation
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean three-surface split: (public) for donor feed, (center) for back-office, (admin) for moderation — each with its own layout, guards, and queries. db/ layer fully decoupled from UI.
architecture.modularity: src/db (schema, queries, admin-queries, jobs), src/lib/auth (requireCenter, requireAdmin, getCurrentCenter), src/lib/registro + solicitudes (validation), src/components/ui (design-system primitives)
notable_patterns: ISR caching with tag-based invalidation (active-requests, landing-stats, request:<id>) for donor surge resilience
Idempotency keys on request inserts to handle double-submit and offline draft support
TOCTOU-safe membership uniqueness via DB unique index + unique-violation catch in the transaction
Append-only moderation_event audit log for all center/request state changes
GitHub Actions schedule triggers cron endpoint every 5 min (workaround for Vercel Hobby cron limitation)
Auto-run Drizzle migrations on Vercel production build via prebuild script, guarded by VERCEL_ENV

code_organization.naming_quality: Consistent English identifiers throughout (DB, code, types); Spanish reserved only for user-facing copy; enums and types all accurately named

Every non-trivial function has a JSDoc explaining invariants and security contracts. Inline comments explain TOCTOU, idempotency, RLS bypass, and caching contracts.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: CRON_SECRET checked fail-closed (missing secret rejects, never collapses to 'Bearer undefined'). SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY is server-only. .env.example documents all vars without leaking values. Local demo keys are correctly called out as public/safe.
production_readiness.security_notes: Server-side authorization at every mutation via requireCenter()/requireAdmin() using JWT-verified getUser(). Client-supplied IDs are never trusted. UUID format validated before DB queries. Phone normalized server-side from verified OTP session only. No open-redirect on login bounce.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: Donor feed is ISR-cached with tag-based invalidation — designed to handle a surge. DB uses Supabase transaction pooler for runtime, direct for migrations. Cron runs via GitHub Actions every 5 min (Vercel Hobby limit); documented path to move to native Vercel cron on Pro.
production_readiness.reasoning: Strong auth, error handling, caching, and deployment automation. Main gap is no structured logging or error observability (no Sentry, no Pino) — silent exceptions in server actions only surface to the user as generic errors.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real — custom domain data model with 5 Drizzle migrations, full business logic, a live deployed URL (venemed.vercel.app), comprehensive e2e tests that exercise real form submissions and server actions against a local ephemeral Supabase stack.

Phase 3 (center workspace slice 3+4: Finalizar/Extender request + reception toggle) is the only meaningful pending feature.

The core loop (center registers → admin approves → center publishes request → donors browse and share) is fully implemented and e2e-tested. Remaining gaps (reception toggle, request extension) don't block the core donation flow.

The time-windowed expiry model and WhatsApp-first auth are specifically designed for Venezuelan conditions. Can be merged into a broader relief portal as the medical-supply coordination surface.
red_flags: No structured logging or error observability (no Sentry, Pino, or equivalent) — silent server action failures reach users only as generic Spanish error messages

VeneMed directly attacks this with time-windowed requests (12/24/48h auto-expiry), real-time visibility of what each center needs, and a surplus-alert to stop over-donation of a specific item. The core coordination loop (center registers → admin approves → center publishes need → donor browses and shares → request auto-expires) is live and functional with real data visible on the homepage.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: venemed.vercel.app loads correctly and shows two active donation requests from "Refugio Casa Esperanza" in Caracas — one for hygiene supplies expiring in 9 hours and one with a surplus alert ("No enviar"). The donor feed, share CTAs, center login, and navigation all render cleanly. This is a real working product, not a landing page or placeholder. The time-windowed expiry mechanism is visibly active.

Lead with the live URL and the real active requests visible on the homepage. Emphasize WhatsApp-first auth as a design decision built for Venezuelan connectivity, not a workaround.

No evidence of real health centers onboarded beyond seed/demo data — social proof from an actual hospital would be powerful
No screenshots prepared for social posts
No license on the repo, which limits open-source narrative

evaluation.one_line_pitch: VeneMed conecta donantes con hospitales venezolanos en tiempo real: los centros publican exactamente lo que necesitan, con una ventana de horas — y cuando se cubre, la solicitud desaparece.

name: Venezuela Juntos (Missing Persons Facial Recognition)
db_description:
## What was built?

  **Venezuela Juntos** is a humanitarian face-matching web app designed to reunite Venezuelan families separated by disaster or displacement.

  ## How it works

Repo: https://github.com/OnBeIt/Venezuela_Juntos_v2

  - Families upload a photo of a missing person with their name
  - Rescuers upload a photo of someone they have found along with a contact phone number or email
  - The system automatically compares faces using AI-powered ArcFace recognition
  - Any pair exceeding the similarity threshold appears on the public Matches page with the rescuer's contact details
  - The app is fully bilingual, Spanish (default) and English

  ## Who is it for?

  Rescuers, volunteers, NGOs, and families searching for loved ones in Venezuelan disaster or displacement scenarios.

  ## Tech stack

  Python · FastAPI · InsightFace (ArcFace) · SQLite · Jinja2 · Tailwind CSS · Docker · Railway

  ## Try it

  Run the app, accept the disclaimer, and use the navigation to report a missing person or register someone who has been found.

analysis.summary: Venezuela Juntos is a face-recognition family reunification web app built for disaster relief. Families upload photos of missing persons; rescuers upload photos of found people with contact info. The app uses InsightFace (ArcFace buffalo_l, 512-d cosine similarity) to automatically cross-match faces and publishes confirmed matches on a public page. The core ML pipeline is genuinely implemented and working — this is not boilerplate. It is a FastAPI server-rendered app with SQLAlchemy/SQLite, containerized with Docker and documented for Railway deployment. The primary blocker for real deployment is the complete absence of auth: all uploaded photos (of vulnerable people) and contact details are publicly accessible to anyone with the URL, which is a serious privacy and safety concern in a real disaster scenario.
analysis.project_type: Web app — AI-powered family reunification via face recognition

architecture.pattern: Monolithic server-rendered MVC
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: SQLAlchemy 2.x DeclarativeBase ORM over SQLite; three tables: missing_persons, found_persons, matches. No migrations (uses create_all on startup).
architecture.api_design: Server-rendered HTML routes via FastAPI + Jinja2; no REST/JSON API. StaticFiles mount for uploaded photos.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Clean three-module split: main.py (routes/controllers), database.py (models + engine), matching.py (embedding + search logic). facerec.py in lib/ is the InsightFace adapter.
architecture.modularity: Small and focused — 4 Python files totaling ~985 LOC. Each module has a single responsibility.
notable_patterns: ThreadPoolExecutor (2 workers) offloads CPU-bound ML inference from the async event loop
Model pre-warmed on startup via lifespan context to avoid first-request timeout
Vectorized O(N) cosine similarity via NumPy matrix-vector multiply (matrix @ probe)
UUID-prefixed filenames for uploads prevent enumeration collisions
i18n data attributes on HTML elements for future localization

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat root with clear module names (main.py, database.py, matching.py), lib/ for the ML adapter, templates/ for Jinja2 views. Data dir created at runtime and gitignored.
code_organization.naming_quality: Excellent — function and variable names are descriptive and consistent (embed, search_pool, embedding_to_blob, get_session, init_db).
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is detailed and accurate: schema, routes, env vars, deployment steps, known limitations all documented. Inline docstrings present in facerec.py.
code_organization.reasoning: For a 985-LOC hackathon project, the organization is notably clean with genuine separation of concerns and no dead code. Minor issue: get_session() returns a bare Session rather than a context manager, requiring manual use of 'with' at call sites.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: Only two env vars needed (DATA_DIR, INSIGHTFACE_HOME) — no secrets required for base operation. No .env file in repo.
production_readiness.security_notes: Critical: the /matches page and /uploads/* static files are fully public — anyone can view all uploaded photos of missing/found victims plus rescuer contact details. No rate limiting on the upload endpoints. No file size cap on uploads. No MIME-type validation (only checks for face detection).
production_readiness.scalability_notes: SQLite with check_same_thread=False is not safe for concurrent writes under load. O(N) full-table embedding scan on every upload will degrade linearly. ThreadPoolExecutor capped at 2 workers means the third concurrent ML request queues. No pagination on /matches.
production_readiness.reasoning: Dockerfile is present and deployment instructions are detailed; env config is clean. However, the complete absence of auth exposes vulnerable people's photos and contact info publicly, which is disqualifying for a real disaster deployment without remediation.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real implementation — the ML pipeline (face embedding, vectorized search, match storage) is fully coded and functional, not placeholder stubs.
maturity.reasoning: 8 commits from 1 contributor over ~24 hours. No tests, no CI, no linting config. README honestly documents known limitations. The Dockerfile works and Railway deployment is documented step-by-step.

viability.reasoning: The core value proposition — automatic face-match between missing-person reports and found-person reports — is genuinely implemented and technically sound. ArcFace is a proven face recognition model. The app could be deployed today and handle low-volume traffic. The 0.40 cosine threshold is permissive and could produce false positives in disaster scenarios (telling a family the wrong person was found), and the privacy/auth gap must be fixed before exposing to real victims.

key_features: ArcFace (InsightFace buffalo_l) face embedding and 1:N cosine-similarity matching
Two-sided upload flows: family reports missing person, rescuer reports found person with contact info
Automatic cross-matching on every upload — new uploads are compared against the entire opposite pool
Public matches page with similarity score bar, side-by-side photos, and rescuer contact info
Loading overlay UI during ML inference (good UX given ~1s CPU inference time)
Docker container with volume-mounted persistent storage; Railway deployment documented
i18n data attributes on HTML for future Spanish/English support
analysis.merge_potential: Unique in the hackathon for its AI face-matching approach to family reunification. No other project is likely doing ArcFace-based cross-matching. Strong candidate to merge or build upon if auth and privacy controls are added. The facerec.py library is reusable in other contexts.
red_flags: No authentication anywhere — all victim photos and rescuer contact details are publicly accessible to anyone who visits the site
0.40 cosine similarity threshold is relatively permissive; false positives in disaster contexts (wrong family match) can cause serious harm
O(N) full embedding scan on every upload — performance degrades linearly with database size
SQLite with check_same_thread=False is not safe for concurrent write load
No file size limit on photo uploads — potential for denial-of-service via large files
No deduplication — same photo uploaded twice creates a self-match
ThreadPoolExecutor limited to 2 workers — third concurrent ML request blocks
No logging — no way to audit who uploaded what or detect abuse

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: Family reunification after earthquakes and displacement is one of the most urgent and emotionally devastating problems in disaster relief. The two-sided upload model — families report missing, rescuers report found — maps directly to how volunteer networks actually operate on the ground. ArcFace-based cross-matching automates what would otherwise require manual photo comparison across hundreds of reports. The problem is real, the frequency in Venezuelan disaster contexts is real, and the approach (AI-assisted face matching) is technically proven. This is not a contrived hackathon scenario.
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The live_url points to the GitHub repository, not a deployed web instance. No publicly accessible running application exists. A demo video is available at https://youtu.be/k4PaAmArSfU, and the project is documented for Railway deployment with Docker, so a working instance can be spun up — but none is currently public-facing.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Position as a technically impressive AI prototype for hackathon audiences and developers: 'ArcFace-powered family reunification — a real ML pipeline built in 24 hours to help find missing persons after Venezuelan disasters.' Emphasize the technology innovation and the humanitarian vision, NOT that victims should use it today. Reserve victim-facing promotion until auth and privacy controls are added.
gaps: No deployed public demo — victims cannot try it today
No authentication means all uploaded victim photos and rescuer contact details are publicly exposed (critical privacy blocker before any real promotion)
No screenshots in the repo for visual assets
0 stars/forks — no community signal yet
Single contributor, 8 commits — needs team expansion for trust

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Venezuela Juntos uses ArcFace AI to automatically match photos of missing persons with found persons — reuniting families separated by disaster in seconds, not days.

name: Venezuela Response Hub
db_description:
## Venezuela Response Hub

Plataforma de coordinación humanitaria construida en respuesta al terremoto de Venezuela (junio 2026), desarrollada durante el ecosistema Build4Venezuela.

### ¿Qué hace?
- Centraliza información verificada de refugios, rutas de evacuación y puntos de ayuda
- Conecta organizaciones humanitarias, voluntarios y afectados en tiempo real
- Integra **SismoLab**, plataforma científica de monitoreo sísmico para Venezuela

### Tecnología
Desarrollado con IA aplicada a coordinación de crisis: procesamiento de datos en tiempo real, geolocalización de recursos y alertas verificadas.

### Impacto
Operativo en horas tras el desastre. Diseñado para escalar a futuras emergencias en América Latina.

### Construido por
Laura Zuluaga- Cofounder & CGO - Young AI Leaders Bogotá Hub · UN AI for Good / ITU Network

analysis.summary: A bilingual (ES/EN) static web hub that curates and links to ~20 community-built tools for the Venezuela earthquake response. Built with TanStack Start / React / Tailwind via the Lovable AI builder in under 48 hours. All content is hardcoded data — there is no database, no backend API, no auth, and no tests. The site is live at vzlaresponsehub.org and serves as an aggregator/directory, not an original tool itself. Value is entirely in curation and bilingual accessibility, not in novel engineering.
analysis.project_type: Static informational hub / curated directory (SSR-capable SPA, deployed as static)

ai_ml: Lovable (AI web builder used for generation)

architecture.pattern: Single-page application with optional SSR via TanStack Start/Nitro. Single route (index.tsx) contains all page logic.
architecture.orm_or_db_layer: None — no database of any kind. All data is hardcoded JS constants (SECTIONS array, EMERGENCY_NUMBERS, donation orgs, i18n dictionaries).
architecture.api_design: None. No API routes. The server.ts file is only a thin SSR error-handling wrapper around TanStack Start's server entry.
architecture.separation_of_concerns: Minimal — the entire application lives in one 1,285-line index.tsx file. A custom i18n module (src/lib/i18n.tsx) separates translation strings. Utility libs are present but mostly unused shadcn scaffolding.
architecture.modularity: Low. All sections (Hero, Tools, Emergency, Push, Prepare, Trust, Footer) are co-located in a single route file. The UI component library is fully scaffolded but only accordion and a few primitives are used.
notable_patterns: Bilingual i18n via custom React context (ES/EN toggle)
Tool trust-level badges (verified/community/unverified) with curated external links
Hardcoded structured data (SECTIONS, QUICK_BUTTONS, EMERGENCY_NUMBERS) — no CMS or dynamic fetch
SSR error normalization in server.ts to handle h3-swallowed errors
Built entirely with Lovable AI builder (306 commits in 2 days)

code_organization.directory_structure: Flat and minimal: src/routes/index.tsx holds all page logic; src/lib/ has i18n, error capture, utils; src/components/ui/ is fully scaffolded shadcn library. No feature folders, no domain separation.
code_organization.naming_quality: Good — component names are descriptive (QuickAccess, ToolCard, TrustBadge, Emergency, Push, Prepare). i18n keys follow a consistent dot-notation pattern.
code_organization.documentation_quality: README is present and accurate at a high level. AGENTS.md exists (intent layer). Source code has section comments. No JSDoc or inline API docs.
code_organization.reasoning: Clean, readable code for a static site of this scope. The single-file pattern is acceptable for the simple use case but would not scale. Lovable-generated boilerplate includes many unused shadcn components.

production_readiness.secrets_handling: No secrets needed — the site has no backend, no API keys in code.
production_readiness.security_notes: All external links use rel='noopener noreferrer'. No user input is processed server-side. WhatsApp group link is hardcoded publicly.
production_readiness.scalability_notes: As a static content site it scales trivially via CDN. However all data updates require a code deploy — there is no CMS, no dynamic data layer, and no way for community contributors to update content without a git push.
production_readiness.reasoning: The site IS live and serves real users, which is meaningful. But it has zero tests, no CI pipeline, no Dockerfile, no env config, and all data is hardcoded requiring deploys for any update. Appropriate for an emergency-built static hub but not production-grade by engineering standards.

maturity.is_real_or_boilerplate: Real content with real humanitarian value, but built heavily on AI-generated scaffolding (Lovable). 306 commits in 2 days is characteristic of AI builder iteration loops, not human engineering commits. The actual custom logic is ~300 lines of i18n strings + ~200 lines of hardcoded tool data.
maturity.reasoning: No tests, no CI, no Dockerfile. The site is deployed and live, which proves some operational maturity. All meaningful 'logic' is static data curation rather than code. Lovable-generated origin means the commit count is misleading.

viability.reasoning: The hub is live at vzlaresponsehub.org and provides genuine value as a bilingual aggregator directing earthquake victims and helpers to the right tools. However, its utility is entirely dependent on the quality of the curated external links, and it cannot be updated without engineering deploys. As a hackathon submission for deploying to help real victims, it is immediately usable but not technically novel.

key_features: Bilingual (Spanish/English) with one-click language toggle
Curated directory of ~20 external community-built tools across 5 categories (people search, maps, comms, shelters, donations)
Trust-level classification for each tool (verified/community/unverified)
Emergency phone numbers for Venezuela (Protección Civil, Bomberos, carriers, ambulance services)
Donation channel directory with drop-off locations across 8+ countries
Earthquake preparedness guide (during/kit/after) with WhatsApp share
Sticky mobile CTA with rotating messages
SEO-optimized with OG tags and canonical URL
Custom SSR error normalization wrapper
analysis.merge_potential: Low. This project is a static directory/hub that links OUT to other tools rather than providing standalone functionality. Its value is in curation and bilingual UX. It could be merged as a reference implementation for a navigation layer or landing page, but it duplicates the "aggregator" role that a main hackathon portal would already serve. The bilingual i18n implementation and trust-badge pattern could be borrowed.
red_flags: All data hardcoded — any content update (new tools, corrected phone numbers, changed drop-off locations) requires a code deploy by the original developer
Built entirely with Lovable AI builder; 306 commits in 2 days is AI iteration noise, not engineering depth
No tests, no CI, no containerization
Hardcoded emergency statistics ('1.430+ fallecidos', '68.900+ desaparecidos') will become stale with no mechanism to update
WhatsApp group link hardcoded in source — if group changes, requires deploy
The 'live' indicator in the header always shows 'En este segundo, hay personas siendo localizadas' — it is not actually live data
No mechanism for community tool submissions (contact email only, no form or CMS)

evaluation.real_problem_reasoning: The June 2026 Venezuela earthquake left 1,430+ dead and 68,900+ missing. Victims, diaspora, and aid workers face an acute coordination problem: dozens of community-built tools exist but are fragmented across social media and informal channels. This hub solves the discoverability and trust problem by curating 22 verified tools in one bilingual (ES/EN) directory with trust-level badges, emergency phone numbers, donation channels, and shelter finders — all organized by the user's immediate need ("I'm looking for someone", "I need shelter", "I want to donate").
evaluation.live_demo_notes: The site loads fully at vzlaresponsehub.org. It presents crisis statistics, 22 curated external tools categorized by use case (missing persons, shelters, damage reporting, medical teleconsult, donations), emergency phone numbers, and international donation channels including fee-free options. The bilingual toggle and trust-level badges function. Critical caveat: the "live" activity indicator ("En este segundo, hay personas siendo localizadas") is hardcoded marketing copy, not real data. Casualty statistics and tool listings will go stale with no CMS or admin interface — any update requires a code deploy by the original developer.

diffusion.recommended_angle: Position as the bilingual front door to Venezuela earthquake relief: one URL that filters out scams, aggregates 22+ community tools, and tells you exactly where to go based on whether you're searching for a missing person, need shelter, want to donate, or are assessing damage. Highlight the trust-badge system and the fact that it went live within hours of the disaster.
gaps: No screenshots shared in submission materials for quick social use
No confirmed real-user testimonials or traffic numbers
The 'live' activity counter is fake — should not be highlighted in promo copy
Hardcoded statistics (deaths, missing) will become inaccurate over days, undercutting credibility if promoted widely after the acute phase

evaluation.one_line_pitch: Venezuela Response Hub: el directorio bilingüe que conecta a víctimas del terremoto y voluntarios globales con más de 22 herramientas verificadas — desaparecidos, albergues, ayuda médica y donaciones sin estafas, en un solo lugar.
